title: Chapter Two: DIFFERENT QUADRANTS, DIFFERENT PEOPLE - PART 3
excerpt: Insights on Kiyosaki's Cashflow Quadrant and wealth building: Chapter Two: DIFFERENT QUADRANTS, DIFFERENT PEOPLE - PART 3
content:
And the third quadrant is the Business Owner.

The “B” type is almost the complete opposite of the “S” type. An S-type person prefers doing everything themselves because they believe no one else can do the job better. A true B-type person thinks differently. They prefer building systems and surrounding themselves with capable people from all four quadrants: E (Employee), S (Self-employed), B (Business Owner), and I (Investor).

For a B, delegation is not a weakness - it is the main strategy. Their mindset is simple: why do everything alone when you can hire skilled people to do it better, faster, and more efficiently? This frees up their time and mental energy to focus on bigger decisions and long-term thinking.

Henry Ford is often used as a real example of this mindset. According to a well-known story, some intellectuals once tried to prove that Ford was not very knowledgeable. Instead of arguing with them, Ford calmly invited them into his office. They began asking him difficult questions. After listening for a while, Ford reached for the telephones on his desk and called in his team of experts. He asked them to answer the questions directly.

Afterward, Ford explained that he prefers hiring smart, educated people so they can handle detailed questions and problems. This allows him to keep his mind free for more important work - mainly thinking and making big decisions. He is also known for the idea: “Thinking is the hardest work there is. That is why so few people engage in it.”

Robert Kiyosaki explains that his “rich dad” admired Henry Ford and people like John D. Rockefeller. Rich dad believed that learning business leadership means understanding both leadership skills and technical business skills. Many people might have one or the other, but to become a strong B, both are necessary. These skills can be learned over time, and business is both a science and an art. For Kiyosaki, learning both never really stops.

When he was young, his rich dad gave him the children’s book Stone Soup by Marcia Brown. This book was used as part of his early training in leadership. Rich dad defined leadership very simply: it is the ability to bring out the best in people.

Because of this, he trained Kiyosaki and his son in practical business skills such as reading financial statements, marketing, sales, accounting, management, production, and negotiation. At the same time, he constantly reminded them that success depends on working well with people and leading them effectively. He often said that technical business skills are not the hardest part - the real challenge is dealing with people.

Kiyosaki still rereads Stone Soup as a reminder. He admits that when things do not go his way, he can sometimes act more like a controlling tyrant than a good leader, so he uses those lessons to correct himself and stay focused on true leadership.

Entrepreneurial Development

A common idea people often repeat is: “I’m going to start my own business.” Many believe that financial security and happiness come from doing something on their own or creating a brand-new product that nobody else has thought of. Because of this belief, they quickly leave their jobs and jump into starting a business.

From Robert Kiyosaki’s perspective, this is often the path many people choose when they are driven by the desire for independence and wealth. However, the key point behind his thinking is that people often act on emotion and assumptions, believing that owning a business automatically leads to success without fully understanding what it actually takes to build and sustain one.

Many people end up starting what is known as an S-type business instead of a B-type business. Neither one is automatically better or worse; each comes with its own advantages, disadvantages, risks, and rewards.

The problem Robert Kiyosaki points out is that many individuals who aim to build a B-type business end up creating an S-type business instead. Because of this, they get stuck and struggle to transition toward the business model they originally wanted.

He also notes that many new entrepreneurs begin with the intention of building something scalable and system-driven, but their actions often lead them into a self-employed structure instead.

But they wind up doing this instead and get stuck there:

Many then attempt to do this:

Only a small number of people who try to move into these areas actually succeed. The reason is simple: each of the four quadrants requires a different set of abilities and a different way of thinking. The skills needed to be an employee are not the same as those needed to run a business or invest.

Success in any one quadrant is not automatic - you must deliberately learn the specific technical skills and develop the right mindset for that quadrant. Without adapting to what that position demands, real success is unlikely.

Stay tuned to learn the difference between an S-type business and a B-type business!

title: Chapter Two: DIFFERENT QUADRANTS, DIFFERENT PEOPLE - PART 2
excerpt: Insights on Kiyosaki's Cashflow Quadrant and wealth building: Chapter Two: DIFFERENT QUADRANTS, DIFFERENT PEOPLE - PART 2
content:
In this chapter, we will take a closer look at the key differences between each quadrant of the CASHFLOW Quadrant.

Robert Kiyosaki often uses the phrase, “Hear their words; feel their souls,” to emphasize that the language people use reflects their deeper values, beliefs, and motivations. He suggests that a person’s attitude toward money and work can often reveal which quadrant they belong to.

The E (Employee)

According to Kiyosaki, employees are typically individuals who prioritize security and stability. Words like “job security,” “benefits,” and “retirement plan” often reflect this mindset.

People in the E quadrant generally prefer predictability in their financial lives. They tend to avoid uncertainty and feel more comfortable with fixed salaries, structured roles, and clearly defined benefits. For them, peace of mind often comes from financial stability rather than high risk or uncertain rewards.

Employees exchange their time and skills for a consistent income. This includes everyone from entry-level workers to top executives—what defines them is not their position, but the fact that they work under a contract and receive compensation from an employer.

The S (Self-Employed)

Kiyosaki describes self-employed individuals as people who prefer independence and control over their work. He refers to them as “do-it-yourselfers.”

Unlike employees who seek security, those in the S quadrant tend to respond to uncertainty by taking full responsibility for their outcomes. They prefer relying on their own abilities rather than depending on a boss or organization.

People in this group believe that effort and skill should directly determine income. They often value autonomy highly and dislike having others decide their worth or work methods. This mindset is common among professionals such as doctors, lawyers, consultants, electricians, artists, real estate agents, and small business owners.

Many self-employed individuals are perfectionists who take pride in their expertise, often believing that no one else can perform their work at the same level. This strong commitment to quality helps them succeed, but it can also make delegation difficult.

As a result, they may end up overworked, since they continue handling most tasks themselves. Even when they train others, those individuals may eventually leave to start their own ventures.

For Kiyosaki, the defining trait of the S quadrant is not the occupation itself, but the desire for independence, control, and freedom in how work is done.

Stay tuned to dive into the other two quadrants.

title: Chapter Two: DIFFERENT QUADRANTS, DIFFERENT PEOPLE - PART 1
excerpt: Insights on Kiyosaki's Cashflow Quadrant and wealth building: Chapter Two: DIFFERENT QUADRANTS, DIFFERENT PEOPLE - PART 1
content:
In this part, Robert Kiyosaki shares an important lesson from Chapter 2: “Different Quadrants, Different People.”

Kiyosaki explains that financial growth is not only about learning business or investment skills. The real challenge is changing your mindset and the way you emotionally respond to money, fear, and failure.

He talks about his educated father, who believed that older people cannot easily change their habits or way of thinking. As time passed, his father realized that many of his life goals were still unfulfilled. Kiyosaki tried to introduce him to the CASHFLOW Quadrant and teach him different ways to earn income, but his father found it difficult to step outside his comfort zone.

Even though his father understood business ideas intellectually, he struggled emotionally when things became risky. After experiencing failure in business and losing money, he returned to the safer employee mindset because it felt more secure and familiar.

Kiyosaki’s rich dad explained that the biggest difference between people is not education or intelligence - it is how they handle fear, uncertainty, and failure. Some people choose security when they feel afraid, while others are willing to take risks in search of financial freedom and opportunity.

He also explains that moving from one quadrant to another is not just about changing careers. It requires a deep personal transformation - changing your habits, beliefs, and the way you see the world. Rich dad compares this process to a caterpillar turning into a butterfly: once you change, your lifestyle, thinking, and even relationships may also change.

Another key lesson is that people reveal their mindset through the words they use:

• Employees usually talk about job security, salary, and benefits.

• Self-employed people focus on fees, commissions, and doing the work themselves.

• Business owners think about systems, teams, and leadership.

• Investors focus on cash flow, returns, and growing money.

Rich dad believed that words are powerful because they show a person’s inner thinking and values. That’s why he taught Kiyosaki the importance of listening carefully. By understanding the words people use, you can better understand their mindset, fears, and goals.

The main message of this chapter is simple: true financial success is not only about knowledge or hard work. It also depends on your mindset, emotional strength, and willingness to change from within.

More valuable lessons are coming next - stay tuned!

title: Chapter One: WHY DON’T YOU GET A JOB? - PART 3
excerpt: Insights on Kiyosaki's Cashflow Quadrant and wealth building: Chapter One: WHY DON’T YOU GET A JOB? - PART 3
content:
Why are you just staring at it? It’s time to learn from Robert’s experiences - valuable insights are waiting for you. Just dive in!

Robert Kiyosaki explains that his two fathers had completely different beliefs about money and success. His highly educated father believed that loving money was wrong and that making large profits meant a person was greedy. Even though he worked hard, was honest, and cared deeply about education, he often said things like, “Money isn’t important,” “I can’t afford it,” and “Investing is risky.” As a result, he stayed financially insecure despite his education and professional success.

In contrast, Kiyosaki’s rich dad believed that money was important because it supported freedom and a better life. He didn’t believe people should spend their entire lives working for money. Instead, he believed in building businesses and investments so that money and systems could work for you. According to him, wealth provided time for family, health, travel, charity, and helping the community. For rich dad, money was not the goal itself - it was a tool that created freedom and opportunities.

Kiyosaki says this difference in mindset shaped his own choices. He became interested in the “B” (Business Owner) and “I” (Investor) side of the CASHFLOW Quadrant because those paths offered long-term financial freedom. Through observing both fathers, he noticed that small differences in thinking about money eventually created huge differences in lifestyle and financial security over time.

One major lesson came from watching his educated father’s career collapse. After standing up against political corruption and losing his government position, his father struggled financially for the rest of his life. Although he was intelligent and respected, he had spent his whole life in the employee world and lacked the skills and mindset needed to succeed in business. His attempts to become self-employed failed, leaving him frustrated and dependent on government support later in life.

Meanwhile, Kiyosaki’s rich dad continued growing wealthier with age because he had spent years building businesses and investments. Instead of working harder for money, he created systems that generated income and gave him more free time.

From these experiences, Kiyosaki concludes that success is not just about choosing a career, but about choosing the kind of person you want to become financially. He argues that schools mainly prepare people to become employees, while the skills needed to succeed as business owners and investors are rarely taught. The main message of the chapter is that each quadrant in the CASHFLOW system represents a different way of thinking, living, and earning money, and understanding these differences can help people choose the financial path that best fits their goals and values.

The next chapter reveals a powerful shift in thinking - don’t miss it!

title: Chapter One: WHY DON’T YOU GET A JOB? - PART 2
excerpt: Insights on Kiyosaki's Cashflow Quadrant and wealth building: Chapter One: WHY DON’T YOU GET A JOB? - PART 2
content:
So, are you ready to discover: "From Which Quadrant Do You Generate Your Income?"

Let’s break it down.

The CASHFLOW Quadrant explains the four different ways people earn money. Robert Kiyosaki describes it as a model that shows how income is generated, not just what job someone does.

In the first quadrant, E (Employee), people earn money by working for a company or an employer. In the S (Self-Employed) quadrant, people earn money by working for themselves, such as freelancers, doctors with private practices, or small business owners who do most of the work themselves.

In the B (Business Owner) quadrant, people build or own systems and businesses that generate income, even when they are not personally doing the day-to-day work. In the I (Investor) quadrant, people make money by investing money into assets like stocks, bonds, or real estate, allowing money itself to generate more money.

Kiyosaki emphasizes that each quadrant requires a different mindset, different skills, and even a different personality. That’s why people are often naturally drawn to certain quadrants and uncomfortable in others. Moving between quadrants can feel as different as switching from one completely different activity to another.

He also explains that most people have the ability to earn income from all four quadrants. The choice of where someone earns most of their money is less about formal education and more about their inner values, strengths, weaknesses, and interests. For example, a doctor could be an employee in a hospital (E), run a private practice (S), own a clinic managed by others (B), and also invest in stocks or real estate (I).

Kiyosaki stresses that it is not just about what people do, but how they generate income. The key idea is “generate income from,” meaning money can come from different sources and systems, not only from working hours.

He also points out that none of the quadrants guarantee success. People can become rich or poor in any of them. However, each quadrant has different advantages. For example, he prefers the B and I quadrants because they offer more opportunities for tax advantages and allow money to work more efficiently without being heavily reduced by taxes.

Ultimately, Kiyosaki’s message is that there are many ways to earn money beyond traditional employment. The CASHFLOW Quadrant is meant to help people understand these options and decide which path fits their goals, mindset, and desired lifestyle.

Now get ready to explore how Robert’s Rich Dad and Poor Dad thought differently about money and wealth.

title: Chapter One: WHY DON’T YOU GET A JOB? - PART 1
excerpt: Insights on Kiyosaki's Cashflow Quadrant and wealth building: Chapter One: WHY DON’T YOU GET A JOB? - PART 1
content:
The first chapter of CASHFLOW QUADRANT begins with a quote. But what does it really mean? Let’s explore it more deeply to understand it clearly.

In 1985, Robert Kiyosaki and his wife Kim were in a very difficult situation. They were homeless, unemployed, and almost out of money. Their credit cards were maxed out, and they were even living in an old Toyota car. After some time, a friend helped them by offering a place to stay in her basement, where they lived for about nine months.

Even though their situation was very bad, they tried to keep it private. On the outside, they still looked normal, so when people found out, they often asked them, “Why don’t you just get a job?” But Kiyosaki explains that it wasn’t easy to answer that question because their goal was not just a job or security - it was financial freedom.

They did small odd jobs here and there just to survive, but they were not focused on building a traditional career. In fact, even though stable jobs would have been easier, they chose to continue struggling toward a bigger goal. He admits there were moments when a steady paycheck sounded comforting, but they kept going.

That year was extremely hard. Money problems created stress, fear, and arguments between him and Kim. But he also says those struggles made their relationship stronger because they stayed committed to the same vision. They knew where they wanted to go, even if they didn’t know exactly how they would get there.

Kiyosaki points out that both of them were educated and could have easily found good jobs. However, they were not interested in job security - they wanted true financial freedom.

By 1989, they had become millionaires. But even then, he says they still weren’t truly financially free. That only happened in 1994, when they reached a point where they no longer needed to work for money at all.

He uses this story to challenge a common belief that you need money to make money. But his experience shows something different. They started with no money and even debt, yet still managed to build wealth over time.

He also questions the idea that formal education is the main key to financial success. Even though he has a college degree, he says it did not play a major role in his financial journey. Subjects like math, physics, and literature were not directly useful in helping him build wealth.

To support his point, he gives examples of successful people like Thomas Edison, Henry Ford, Bill Gates, and Steve Jobs, who became very successful without following traditional education paths. His main idea is that understanding business and money is more important than formal education for building wealth.

So, what does it really take to become financially free, according to Kiyosaki?

He says it starts with having a clear goal, strong determination, willingness to learn quickly, and using your natural skills in the right way. Most importantly, it means understanding how you earn money and knowing where you fit in what he calls the CASHFLOW Quadrant.

The CASHFLOW Quadrant shows four ways people earn money:

• E (Employee): Works for a salary

• S (Self-employed): Works for themselves

• B (Business owner): Owns systems and businesses that earn money

• I (Investor): Makes money through investments

His main message is simple: most people stay on the “E” or “S” side, but real financial freedom happens when you move to the “B” and “I” side - where money works for you instead of you working for money.

So have you ever thought about which quadrant your income belongs to? Let’s find out next - stay tuned!

title: Introduction: WHICH QUADRANT ARE YOU IN?
excerpt: Insights on Kiyosaki's Cashflow Quadrant and wealth building: Introduction: WHICH QUADRANT ARE YOU IN?
content:
Yeah, we’re back again with another powerful book: CASHFLOW Quadrant. 

Just dive in to discover informative insights that not only inspire dreams, but also show the way to achieve them.

This is not just a book about money - it’s a book about freedom. It teaches why some people work their whole lives yet still feel stuck, while others build income that continues to grow even when they are not working.

Robert Kiyosaki’s CASHFLOW Quadrant explains money in a very practical way. He says the reason many people work their whole lives but still don’t feel free is because they only know one way to earn money.

He divides people into four categories, based on where their income comes from. 

First is E - Employee

This is someone who works for a company and gets a paycheck every month. It feels safe because there is salary, benefits, and stability. But the problem is simple - if they stop working, the income usually stops too. 

Second is S - Self-Employed 

This person works for themselves. Think of doctors, lawyers, freelancers, shop owners, consultants. They have more control than employees, but many are still trapped in work. If they don’t show up, business slows down. 

So both Employees and Self-Employed people often trade time for money. 

Third is B - Business Owner 

This is different. A business owner builds a system. They create a company where people, processes, and teams work together. The business can keep earning even when the owner is not involved every minute. 

Fourth is I - Investor 

This is where money starts working for the person. Investors put money into assets like stocks, real estate, or businesses, and those assets generate more income. 

Kiyosaki says most people stay on the left side: 

• Employee

• Self-employed

Where they personally work for money. 

But wealthy people usually move to the right side: 

• Business owner

• Investor

Where money works for them. 

The Famous Story: Buckets vs Pipeline

He tells a simple story. Two men got a contract to bring water to a village.

The first man, Ed

He carried buckets from the lake every day. He worked from morning to night. The harder he worked, the more he earned.

But if he got sick, tired, or stopped working, money stopped. 

The second man, Bill

Instead of carrying buckets, he spent time planning. He found investors, built a company, hired workers, and created a pipeline from the lake to the village.

Now water flowed every day automatically.

Bill built a system once - and got paid again and again.

That’s the real question of the book:

• Are you carrying buckets… or building pipelines?

• Are you only working for income today?

• Or are you creating something that keeps paying you tomorrow?

What Pipelines Look Like Today

A pipeline could be:

• A business with employees

• Rental income

• Investments

• A YouTube channel

• Digital products

• Royalties

• A brand

• Equity in companies

Something that can earn without needing all your daily time.

Why People Stay Stuck

Many people don’t move because of:

• Fear of losing security

• Fear of failure

• No money education

• Comfort zone

• Depending on one paycheck

What He Wants us to Understand

A job is not bad. Self-employment is not bad. But depending on only active income can keep you stuck.

Salary can pay bills. Assets can build freedom.

Use earned income to build businesses, investments, and assets. Because true freedom begins when your money keeps coming in, even when you’re not working every hour for it.

So from the next post onward, let’s go deeper chapter by chapter and understand what Robert Kiyosaki is really trying to teach us.

title: An Overview of Rich Dad Poor Dad
excerpt: Insights on Kiyosaki's Cashflow Quadrant and wealth building: An Overview of Rich Dad Poor Dad
content:
Finally, let’s take a step back and reflect on what Rich Dad Poor Dad by Robert Kiyosaki really teaches us.

Yes, it all started with the small spark of a young boy who wanted to become rich. It began with a powerful question: “Dad, can you tell me how to get rich?”

By now, the answer feels clearer - it’s not just about income, but about mindset and financial education.

Throughout the book, we discover that the rich don’t work for money… money works for them.

And this idea is built on a few core concepts:

• Financial literacy is everything - knowing the difference between assets and liabilities can completely change your life.

• Mind your own business - focus on building assets, not just earning a salary.

• The rich think differently - they use systems like taxes and corporations to grow and protect their wealth.

• Opportunities are created, not found - the more you learn, the more you see.

• Work to learn, not just to earn - skills and knowledge are your biggest assets. But is that all the book offers? Not even close.

But is that all the book offers? Not even close.

Kiyosaki contrasts two mindsets through “Rich Dad” and “Poor Dad,” showing how upbringing and beliefs shape our financial future. One teaches security and stability… the other teaches risk-taking and opportunity.

He also explains how fear and greed influence many financial decisions - fear of losing money keeps people stuck, while the desire for comfort leads to poor choices. Recognizing these emotions is key to overcoming them.

Another powerful takeaway is the importance of taking risks and learning from failure. The rich aren’t afraid of mistakes - they see them as lessons.

He also emphasizes the value of building businesses and investments instead of relying solely on a paycheck. Ownership, not employment, is often the path to financial freedom.

And then comes a simple but life-changing idea: “Don’t say ‘I can’t afford it.’ Ask, ‘How can I afford it?’” That small shift trains your brain to find solutions instead of excuses.

In the end, Rich Dad Poor Dad isn’t just a book about money. It’s about breaking old patterns, thinking differently, and taking control of your financial future.

Because dreaming about wealth is easy - but building it requires learning, action, and the courage to think differently.

Next, we take this understanding a step further with Cashflow Quadrant - where Kiyosaki explains how different people earn money… and how you can move toward true financial freedom.

title: Final Thoughts from Robert Kiyosaki
excerpt: Insights on Kiyosaki's Cashflow Quadrant and wealth building: Final Thoughts from Robert Kiyosaki
content:
So basically, Robert Kiyosaki is saying this:

Most of us are taught the same thing growing up - study well, get a job, work hard, save money, maybe take loans when needed. But he says that way of thinking doesn’t really work anymore.

Instead of just working harder, we should start thinking smarter about money.

Like, a lot of people worry about things like their kids’ education or retirement. Normally, they think, “I need to work more and save more.” But Kiyosaki says - no, the real solution is to use money wisely.

He even gives an example. One of his friends was saving a little money every month for his kids’ college. That would take a long time, right? So Kiyosaki suggested investing instead. The friend bought a small property, rented it out, and started earning extra income. Slowly, that income grew, and it became much easier for him to handle those big expenses.

So the main point is - money is really about how you think.

If you want more money, you have to change your mindset.

He also explains that there are three types of income:

• One is the money you work for (like your salary)

• Second is money that comes without working much (like rent)

• Third is money from investments (like stocks)

Most people only depend on salary. But rich people focus more on the other two - where money works for them.

He also says people fall into four groups - employees, self-employed, business owners, and investors. And usually, real wealth comes from being a business owner or investor.

Another important thing he says is - investing is not actually risky. What’s really risky is not knowing what you’re doing.

And finally, he gives a simple but powerful idea:

Every time you earn money, you have a choice - you can spend it and stay stuck, or spend it on useless things and stay average, or invest it and slowly build wealth.

He reminds readers that their financial future depends on the choices they make today. By learning, thinking differently, and making smarter decisions, anyone can work toward financial freedom.

He concludes by wishing readers success, wealth, and happiness, encouraging them to use their knowledge to create a better life for themselves and their families.

title: Chapter 9: STILL WANT MORE? HERE ARE SOME TO DOS - Part 2
excerpt: Insights on Kiyosaki's Cashflow Quadrant and wealth building: Chapter 9: STILL WANT MORE? HERE ARE SOME TO DOS - Part 2
content:
Do you have any guesses about what this chapter holds? Let’s dive in!

Robert Kiyosaki once explains how he teaches people to invest in apartment houses. He takes a friend and her agent to look at six properties. Most are bad, but two are good. He suggests making offers on all six - about half the asking price.

The friend and her agent are shocked. They think it is rude and will offend the sellers, so they do nothing. They walk away and keep searching for a “better deal.” But no offers are made, and she is still searching today.

Kiyosaki’s point is that you never truly know the right price until someone makes an offer and the seller responds. Most sellers ask for too much, but rarely too little. To find real opportunities, you must participate and make offers.

He believes buying and selling is like a game. It should not be taken too seriously. Many people overthink it and miss chances. The key is to make offers, negotiate, face rejection, and keep going.

He often uses flexible “escape clauses” in deals so he can back out if needed. He even jokes that his business partner is his cat, to show how simple and flexible deal-making can be if people stop overcomplicating it.

For Kiyosaki, investing is like dating - you must go out, meet people, explore opportunities, make offers, and accept rejection as part of the process. Waiting at home for the perfect deal rarely works.

He also stresses observation and consistency. By regularly walking, jogging, or driving through neighborhoods, he studies small changes over time. Real profit comes from two things: finding a bargain and noticing change early.

He looks for signs like long-unsold properties, moving trucks, new businesses, or shifting neighborhood activity. Even a few minutes of monthly observation can reveal opportunities.

Kiyosaki also compares everyday shopping with investing. People happily buy when supermarkets have discounts, but they run away when real estate or stocks go on “sale.” In his view, profits are made when you buy, not when you sell.

He emphasizes buying smart and looking in the right places. Some people overpay through traditional channels, while others find the same deals through foreclosures or better research.

Another key lesson is to think bigger. Instead of acting alone or thinking small, he suggests combining resources, negotiating in groups, and aiming for larger deals. Bigger thinking creates bigger opportunities.

He gives examples of structuring deals creatively - like securing land, then selling portions while keeping profit or even getting his share free.

He reminds readers that many successful companies and people started small and succeeded over time.

In the end, Robert Kiyosaki’s message is clear: action is everything. Opportunities only appear when you participate. Make offers, take risks, learn from rejection, and keep moving forward - because success comes from doing, not waiting.

We’re almost at the end of this book review - stay tuned for the final points.

title: Chapter 9 STILL WANT MORE? HERE ARE SOME TO DOS - Part 1
excerpt: Insights on Kiyosaki's Cashflow Quadrant and wealth building: Chapter 9 STILL WANT MORE? HERE ARE SOME TO DOS - Part 1
content:
Many people read books, browse websites, or attend seminars looking for the perfect formula to achieve financial freedom. But knowing isn’t enough - most people never take action.

In this chapter, Robert Kiyosaki shares a practical “to-do” guide based on how he personally got started. It’s for those ready to think and act.

Kiyosaki emphasizes that understanding the philosophy behind investing is just as important as taking action. Some people act without thinking, while others think but never act. He considers himself both - a thinker and a doer.

Here’s a summary of his actionable steps:

1. Stop what isn’t working.

Kiyosaki advises taking a break to assess your current actions. Doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results is useless. Identify what isn’t working, stop it, and look for something new.

2. Seek out new ideas.

He often visits bookstores to find unique investment ideas, even buying books on subjects he knows nothing about. For example, he read The 16 Percent Solution by Joel Moskowitz and immediately acted on it the next Thursday. He ignores naysayers, like neighbors who try to convince him it won’t work, because they lack real experience.

3. Learn from those who have done it.

Kiyosaki suggests finding someone experienced in what you want to do and learning from them directly. For his 16 percent tax-lien investments, he went to the county tax office, found a knowledgeable government employee, invited her to lunch, and learned everything she knew. By the next day, he had acquired two properties earning 16 percent interest.

4. Take classes, read, and attend seminars.

He actively searches for free or paid courses, both online and offline, and invests in seminars to expand his knowledge. Kiyosaki attributes his financial freedom to these educational experiences. He contrasts this with friends who refused to learn and remained stuck in their jobs.

5. Make lots of offers and take action.

When pursuing real estate, he evaluates multiple properties and makes offers, leaving the specifics to real estate agents. He emphasizes taking action quickly and efficiently, rather than overthinking every detail.

Overall, Kiyosaki’s approach blends thinking and doing: he constantly learns, seeks advice from experts, tests new ideas, and acts decisively. He highlights that success comes from both understanding the philosophy and taking concrete action, often in small but consistent steps.

And there’s more - Kiyosaki also shares a story about a friend that will teach even more practical lessons. Stay tuned to discover it.

title: Chapter 8: Getting Started - Part 9
excerpt: Insights on Kiyosaki's Cashflow Quadrant and wealth building: Chapter 8: Getting Started - Part 9
content:
And the final step to awakening our financial genius is: “Teach and you shall receive - the power of giving.”

Let’s dive deeper and learn beyond the trap. Let’s choose real wealth!

Both of Robert Kiyosaki’s dads were teachers, but they taught him very different lessons about giving. His “rich dad” taught him the importance of being generous - not just with knowledge, but also with money. His “educated dad” gave his time and wisdom freely but rarely gave money, often saying he would give only when he had extra, which was almost never.

Rich dad believed in giving first, whether to his church, a charity, or someone in need. He often said, “If you want something, you first need to give.” Kiyosaki learned that giving what you want - money, kindness, help, or friendship, creates a flow that comes back to you, often multiplied. He realized this works almost like magic: give sales help, and sales come; give contacts, and contacts come; give love or a smile, and it returns.

Rich dad also pointed out that “poor people are more greedy than rich people,” because rich people focus on providing what others need. Whenever Kiyosaki felt he was lacking something, he tried to find it within himself to give it first and it always returned in some form. He compared it to a man holding firewood on a cold night, waiting for heat from the stove before adding the wood. In life, money, love, happiness, and opportunity work best when you give first.

Even thinking about what you want, and how you can give it to others - can open doors. Smiling at people often brings smiles back. Teaching what you know brings learning and growth. Sometimes, giving may not bring immediate results, but when it is done with genuine intent, the benefits eventually appear.

Kiyosaki’s dads showed that generosity - whether of knowledge, money, or time, makes people wiser and more successful. Being generous connects you to greater opportunities, and success becomes easier when you share what you have.

Stay tuned for the next chapter!

title: Chapter 8: Getting Started - Part 8
excerpt: Insights on Kiyosaki's Cashflow Quadrant and wealth building: Chapter 8: Getting Started - Part 8
content:
Step Nine: Choose Heroes – The Power of Myth

As a kid, Robert Kiyosaki had heroes - baseball legends like Willie Mays, Hank Aaron, and Yogi Berra. He didn’t just watch them play; he wanted to be them. He collected their baseball cards, memorized every stat, and followed their journey from the minor leagues to the big leagues.

And when he stepped onto the field himself? He wasn’t just Robert. He was his hero, swinging the bat or catching the ball as if he were one of the greats.

That’s the magic of heroes: pretending to be someone you admire is one of the most powerful ways to learn. And yet, most of us forget this as we grow up.

Even today, Kiyosaki sees it happening - kids on the basketball court aren’t just playing, they’re acting like their favorite stars. Copying heroes isn’t just play; it’s real learning.

As he got older, his heroes evolved - golfers, business leaders, and investors like Donald Trump, Warren Buffett, Peter Lynch, George Soros, and Jim Rogers. Just like he memorized baseball stats as a kid, he studied their strategies, deals, and investments.

When negotiating a deal or analyzing the market, he channels their mindset, thinking and acting with their confidence. Heroes give him access to their “raw genius.”

The best part?

Heroes don’t just inspire - they make the hard stuff look easy. And when something looks easy, it sparks a powerful thought: “If they can do it, so can I.” In investing, too many people make it seem impossible. Kiyosaki’s tip: find heroes who make it look easy, and learn from them.

Stay tuned for the final scoop of this chapter - you won’t want to miss it!

title: Chapter 8: Getting Started - Part 7
excerpt: Insights on Kiyosaki's Cashflow Quadrant and wealth building: Chapter 8: Getting Started - Part 7
content:
Let’s talk about the eighth step: “Use assets to buy luxuries: the power of focus.”

A friend’s 16-year-old son wanted a car. He said all his friends’ parents had given theirs a car, and he even offered to use his own savings for a down payment. His father didn’t know whether to just buy the car or let him use his money. Kiyosaki’s advice was clear: buying the car would solve the problem now, but it wouldn’t teach the boy anything about money. Instead, the father could turn the boy’s desire into a lesson about financial skills.

Two months later, the father told Kiyosaki what he did. He gave his son $3,000 - but with rules. First, they played the CASHFLOW game and talked about smart money habits. Then he gave him books about investing and a subscription to the Wall Street Journal. The catch? He couldn’t buy a car directly. He had to use the $3,000 to trade stocks. Only after turning it into $6,000 could he buy the car.

At first, the boy lost some money - but it sparked his interest. He studied, read more, and followed the market carefully. Even though he was down $2,000, he was learning, experimenting, and getting excited about investing. The father said it was the best $3,000 he ever spent - because the boy was learning how money works and building respect for it.

Kiyosaki uses this story to teach a bigger lesson: financial success isn’t just about earning money, it’s about having the discipline and intelligence to make money work for you. Many people spend their money on things they want, instead of investing or creating assets, which leads to financial struggle. For example, if 100 people were given $10,000:

• 80 would spend it all, often going into debt.

• 16 might grow it 5 -10%.

• 4 could turn it into much more.

School teaches us to work for money, but it’s just as important to learn how to make money work for us. Kiyosaki emphasizes that wants, like a luxury car, should drive smart financial choices, not instant gratification. Borrowing money is easy short-term but can be costly long-term. Investing and building assets may take effort at first but pays off later.

The key is financial intelligence: you need to be smarter than money. When you are, money follows your direction instead of controlling you. Without this skill, you’ll spend your life working for money. Learning this early lays the foundation for true financial freedom.

Next up: the ninth step!

title: Chapter 8: Getting Started – Part 7
excerpt: Insights on Kiyosaki's Cashflow Quadrant and wealth building: Chapter 8: Getting Started – Part 7
content:
Let’s look at the seventh step.

Step 7: Be an Indian giver - the power of getting something for nothing.

When the first European settlers arrived in America, they noticed a cultural habit among some American Indians that confused them. If a settler was cold, an Indian might lend them a blanket. The settler often thought it was a gift. But later, when the Indian asked for the blanket back, the settler felt offended. At the same time, the Indians were surprised when the settlers refused to return it. This simple cultural misunderstanding is where the term “Indian giver” came from.

Robert Kiyosaki explains that in the world of investing, thinking like an “Indian giver” can actually help you build wealth. Smart investors always ask an important question:

“How fast can I get my money back?”

They also look for something extra - something they can keep even after getting their original money back. This is why ROI is so important.

For example, Kiyosaki once found a small condominium that was in foreclosure near his home. The bank wanted $60,000 for it, but he offered $50,000 and included a cashier’s check to show he was serious. The bank accepted his offer.

Some investors might say paying cash is risky because it ties up too much money. But in this case, Kiyosaki knew the property had good income potential. His company used the condo as a vacation rental. During the winter months, when tourists called “snowbirds” visit Arizona, the condo rented for about $2,500 per month for four months. During the off-season, it rented for about $1,000 per month.

In about three years, he recovered his entire investment. After that, the condo continued generating income every month. In simple terms, he got his money back and still owned the asset.

He says he uses a similar approach with stocks. Sometimes his broker suggests a company that may soon increase in value. Kiyosaki invests money for a short time - maybe a week or a month, while the price goes up. Once he earns enough, he pulls out his original investment.

What remains are shares that he now owns for free. Since his original money is already back, he can use it to invest in another opportunity.

Of course, not every investment works. Kiyosaki admits that out of ten investments, two or three might be very successful, five or six might do nothing special, and two or three might lose money. But he controls the risk by investing only money he can afford to lose.

People who fear risk usually keep their money in the bank. Saving money is good, but bank savings grow slowly and rarely give you anything extra.

Kiyosaki says that in every investment he looks for two things:

• Limited risk

• A possible bonus asset

That bonus might be extra property, shares, land, or another valuable benefit that remains after he gets his original money back.

He also shares the example of Ray Kroc, the man who built McDonald’s into a global brand. Ray Kroc was not just interested in selling hamburgers. What he really wanted was the real estate where the McDonald’s restaurants were located. By doing this, he gained valuable property along with the business.

The lesson is simple: Smart investors don’t just look at profits. They look at how quickly they can recover their original money and what valuable assets they can still keep afterward.

Developing this ability to see opportunities and hidden value is what Robert Kiyosaki calls financial intelligence.

Stay tuned to learn about the eighth step!

title: Chapter 8: Getting Started - Part 6
excerpt: Insights on Kiyosaki's Cashflow Quadrant and wealth building: Chapter 8: Getting Started - Part 6
content:
Ready for the final takeaway on “Pay Yourself First: The Power of Self-Discipline”?

Robert Kiyosaki teaches that one of the biggest financial mistakes people make is buying things they cannot truly afford. Instead of rushing to purchase a large house or a luxury car, he believes the focus should be on building assets - things that put money into your pocket. Keep your expenses low. Grow your income-producing investments first. Otherwise, you risk getting stuck in what he calls the “Rat Race,” working nonstop just to cover bills.

He also shares an interesting mindset shift: when money feels tight, don’t immediately dip into your savings. Instead, let that pressure challenge you to think differently. Let it push you to find smarter, more creative ways to earn more income. Kiyosaki believes that financial pressure can awaken your “financial genius.” He admits he has faced money problems many times - but instead of touching his investments, he forced himself to figure out new ways to generate income.

According to him, using savings to pay bills is a habit that keeps people stuck. Wealthy individuals use their savings to create more money, not to cover expenses. Yes, this approach sounds strict. But he believes discipline builds strength, and financial strength creates freedom.

At the same time, he makes one thing clear: “pay yourself first” does not mean depriving yourself or living miserably. Life is meant to be enjoyed. The goal is to grow richer while still enjoying the journey. With financial intelligence and creativity, you can pay your bills, build wealth, and live well.

Now, let’s talk about the sixth step: “Pay Your Brokers Well: The Power of Good Advice.”

Kiyosaki says when he sees “For Sale by Owner” signs or hears about “discount brokers,” he remembers what his rich dad taught him: don’t try to save money by avoiding professionals. Instead, invest in the right experts.

His philosophy is simple. If someone is truly a professional, their advice should make you more money than they cost you. That’s why he hires skilled attorneys, accountants, real estate brokers, and stockbrokers. When they succeed, he succeeds.

We live in the Information Age, and good information is powerful. A great broker does more than close deals - they educate you and give you insights you wouldn’t easily find on your own. Kiyosaki values brokers who took the time to teach him when he had very little money. He stayed loyal to them because their knowledge helped him grow.

He also values time. A strong broker acts as his “eyes and ears” in the market, watching opportunities daily so he doesn’t have to. Instead of spending hours tracking every move, he can focus on bigger strategies - or even enjoy a round of golf.

But he gives a warning: not all brokers are the same. Some are only salespeople. He believes there is a huge difference between someone who sells investments and someone who actually owns them. When choosing professionals, he asks what they personally invest in and how they manage their own money. He wants advisors who practice what they teach.

He also believes relationships should be fair. If you constantly focus on cutting someone’s commission, why would they go the extra mile for you? The best professional partnerships benefit both sides.

In the end, Kiyosaki connects this lesson to financial intelligence. One of the most important skills is knowing how to work with - and reward, people who are smarter than you in certain areas. Many people are comfortable managing those below them. True success, however, comes from surrounding yourself with experts and valuing their expertise.

Just like companies have boards of directors, you should build your own team of trusted advisors. That is real financial intelligence.

Curious about the seventh step? Stay tuned!

title: Chapter 8: Getting Started - Part 5
excerpt: Insights on Kiyosaki's Cashflow Quadrant and wealth building: Chapter 8: Getting Started - Part 5
content:
Do you remember that diagram we talked about earlier?

Before we jump back into it, let’s pause for a moment. First, we need to understand why paying yourself first even matters. Once that’s clear, the difference in the diagram will make complete sense.

So, what happens when you put yourself first?

The diagram shows how financially smart people handle money. The moment they earn it, they set aside money for assets first. Only after that do they think about bills and daily expenses.

Many people have heard the famous advice from The Richest Man in Babylon: “Pay yourself first.” But knowing the phrase and actually following it are two very different things. In real life, most people pay their bills first and hope there’s something left to save. Most of the time, there isn’t.

Robert Kiyosaki understands why people push back against this idea. Paying bills first feels “responsible.” And he’s not saying you should ignore your responsibilities. What he’s saying is simple: follow the rule exactly as it’s meant to be followed.

Pay yourself first.

Now think back to the diagram again. Can you spot what’s different this time?

Here’s how Robert explains it.

Robert Kiyosaki believes most people stay stuck financially because they don’t truly understand cash flow. They work hard for decades, but the moment the paycheck stops, so does their income. That’s why so many people depend on support like Social Security later in life.

His core message is simple but powerful: pay yourself first. Unfortunately, most people - even financial professionals - do the opposite. They pay taxes, bills, loans, and everyone else first. Whatever remains, if anything, goes to savings.

Kiyosaki admits there were times when his expenses were higher than his income. Still, he paid himself first. His advisors warned him about the IRS, bad credit, and unpaid bills. But he stayed calm. Why? Because he believed in discipline and inner strength - lessons he learned from The Richest Man in Babylon.

He points out how fear controls most financial decisions. A bill arrives, and panic sets in. A credit card offer shows up, and people swipe without thinking. Society tells us certain debt is “normal,” and we accept it without question. Kiyosaki says real wealth comes from having the courage to think differently and say no.

At the same time, he’s clear about one thing: this isn’t about being reckless. He avoids consumer debt and unnecessary spending. Even though he owns big liabilities, he doesn’t pay for them out of pocket - his assets do. For example, rental income covers property costs.

His golden rule? Avoid consumer debt altogether. Keep your personal expenses low and manageable. That way, paying yourself first doesn’t feel impossible.

And when money feels tight? Kiyosaki still pays himself first and lets the pressure build. Instead of giving in, he uses that pressure as fuel to find new ways to earn and grow money. He refuses to sell investments or drain savings just to cover short-term expenses.

That’s the mindset shift. And if you want the final takeaway from all this - stay tuned. The best part is coming!

title: Chapter 8: Getting Started - Part 4
excerpt: Insights on Kiyosaki's Cashflow Quadrant and wealth building: Chapter 8: Getting Started - Part 4
content:
And the fifth step is: Pay yourself first - the power of self-discipline.

No matter the context, self-discipline plays a central role in everything, right? Let’s see what Kiyosaki has to say about it!

“If you can’t control yourself, don’t try to get rich. It doesn’t make sense to earn money only to spend it carelessly.”

Lack of self-discipline is why many lottery winners go broke and why people who get a raise often splurge instead of saving. Of all the steps to financial success, mastering self-discipline is probably the hardest, but also the most important. It’s the key difference between the rich, the middle class, and the poor. People who can’t handle financial pressure or control themselves will never become truly wealthy. The world isn’t out to get you - it simply exposes those who lack internal control. People without self-discipline often fall victim to those who have it.

In his entrepreneur classes, Kiyosaki emphasizes that success isn’t just about your product or service - it’s about managing three things well:

1. Cash flow – knowing where money comes from and where it goes

2. People – leading and working with others effectively

3. Personal time – using your time wisely

These skills matter in business, family, charity, or life in general, and they all rely on self-discipline.

He also stresses the importance of the advice, “Pay yourself first,” from The Richest Man in Babylon. Millions know the phrase, but few actually practice it. Financial literacy lets you read numbers, and those numbers quickly show who truly follows this advice and who doesn’t.

Robert often says, “A picture is worth a thousand words.” He likes to show people financial statements to make a point. When he compares someone who pays themselves first with someone who doesn’t, the difference is clear.

Look at the diagrams carefully. The important part isn’t just the numbers - it’s how money moves in and out. Most people just glance at the figures and miss the lesson. Cash flow shows who is actually building wealth and who is just working to pay bills. Understanding this is the key to being smart with money.

So, what are these diagrams really telling us? Want to understand them better? Stay tuned!

title: Chapter 8: Getting Started - Part 3
excerpt: Insights on Kiyosaki's Cashflow Quadrant and wealth building: Chapter 8: Getting Started - Part 3
content:
So, what comes next?

Out of the ten steps, number three stands out as the most powerful: choosing your friends wisely.

Who you surround yourself with shapes who you become. This is the power of association. Let’s dive into what Robert Kiyosaki has to say about it!

Kiyosaki doesn’t choose friends based on their wealth. He has friends who live very modestly and others who earn millions. The key for him is learning from everyone.

Sometimes, he intentionally seeks out people with money, but not for their money. He wants their knowledge. Often, these people become close friends. Wealthy friends love talking about money - not to brag, but because they are genuinely interested. Kiyosaki learns from them, and they learn from him.

He also learns from friends who struggle financially. By observing their mistakes, he knows what not to do. Interestingly, billionaires he knows notice that poor friends rarely ask how they built their wealth, they usually ask for a loan or a job.

Kiyosaki warns against taking financial advice from fearful or poor-minded friends. They tend to be “Chicken Littles,” always predicting disaster. While it’s natural to love them, listening to constant doom-and-gloom can be dangerous. Instead, stay open-minded. For example, TV financial experts often disagree - some say the market will crash, others say it will boom. The smart approach is to listen to both perspectives and think for yourself.

Many friends may try to discourage bold investments. Kiyosaki recalls a friend excited about a 6% certificate of deposit. He explained that he earns 16% elsewhere. The friend sent warnings about risk, but Kiyosaki kept earning 16% while the friend stayed at 6%.

One of the hardest parts of building wealth, he says, is staying true to yourself and not following the crowd. In investing, the crowd often arrives late and suffers losses. Good investors look for new opportunities, like surfers searching for the next wave. They don’t try to time the market; they position themselves for the next opportunity.

Smart investors buy when opportunities are unpopular. Profits are made when you buy, not when you sell. They patiently wait for the right moment.

Kiyosaki explains that much of wealth is about information - “legal insider trading,” in a sense. Knowing about the next big boom early gives you a chance for high profits with less risk. That’s why having rich friends is important, they provide access to knowledge, opportunities, and financial intelligence.

The fourth step: Master a formula, then learn a new one – the power of learning quickly

Just like a baker follows a recipe to make bread, people follow a “recipe” to make money - even if it’s one they carry only in their heads. Robert Kiyosaki reminds us that our minds are powerful:

“You become what you study.” What we put into our minds shapes what we do. Study cooking, and you become a cook. Study investing, and your actions reflect that knowledge.

Most people follow the same basic formula for money they learned in school: work for money, pay bills, balance accounts, maybe invest a little, and repeat. That’s the standard recipe, but if you want different results, you need a different formula.

Kiyosaki shares his own experience. At 26, he took a class on buying real estate foreclosures. He learned a formula, but the key was discipline - actually using what he learned. For three years, while working his regular job, he spent spare time mastering that formula. Eventually, it earned him millions.

Even after mastering one formula, he sought out others. Some classes he didn’t use directly, but they still taught him valuable lessons that made his investments more successful. He even learned from experts far beyond his own field, showing that you don’t need to be a specialist to gain new insights.

While community colleges can teach basic financial skills, Kiyosaki always looks for faster, more effective formulas. Today, making more in a day than many earn in a lifetime is possible for those who learn quickly. In a fast-changing world, it’s less about what you already know and more about how fast you can learn. That ability, to discover new “recipes” for making money - is priceless.

The old formula of working hard for money is outdated. The new way is to learn, adapt, and apply smarter formulas to build wealth.

Stay tuned to learn what the fifth step is!

title: Chapter 8: Getting Started - Part 2
excerpt: Insights on Kiyosaki's Cashflow Quadrant and wealth building: Chapter 8: Getting Started - Part 2
content:
Let’s dive into how we can unlock the unique powers we were born with - abilities that only we can control and shape

Step 1: Find a Reason Bigger Than Reality - The Power of Spirit

Most people want to be rich or financially free, but when they see the long, challenging road ahead, it feels easier to just work for money and hand the rest to someone else. Kiyosaki shares the story of a young woman training to swim for the U.S. Olympic team. She got up at 4 a.m., trained for three hours, kept her grades up, and gave up parties. When asked what motivated her, she said it was love - for herself and the people she cared about - that pushed her through the sacrifices.

Kiyosaki explains that a strong reason or purpose is crucial. It comes from a mix of “wants” and “don’t wants.” His “don’t wants” include not wanting to work all his life, not wanting a 9 to 5 like his parents, and not wanting to have his hard work taken away by taxes. His “wants” include freedom to travel, control over his time, living a lifestyle he loves, and having money work for him.

These emotional reasons kept him going through setbacks and failures. He wanted financial freedom by 40 but achieved it at 47, learning along the way. Kiyosaki stresses that without a deep reason or purpose, any path in life will feel much harder.

Step 2: Make Daily Choices - The Power of Choice.

Robert Kiyosaki emphasizes that choice is the core reason people value freedom. Financially, every dollar we earn gives us the power to choose our future - whether to be rich, poor, or middle class. Our spending and learning habits reflect our choices. Kiyosaki credits his love of Monopoly and guidance from his “rich dad” in teaching him the difference between assets and liabilities, helping him choose early to focus on acquiring real assets.

Most people avoid becoming rich because it feels like too much work, often saying, “I’m too young” or “I’ll think about it later.” But Kiyosaki stresses that time and learning are precious assets, and even without money, one can choose to learn.

Education is the most important asset. Instead of buying investments blindly, one should first invest in learning - reading, attending seminars, and listening to experts. Kiyosaki shares how a single $385 seminar in 1973 earned him millions and financial freedom. He uses CDs, audiobooks, and seminars to understand multiple perspectives from successful investors like Buffett, Soros, and Trump, allowing him to approach problems with more than one viewpoint.

He warns against arrogance, which prevents learning and taking risks. True intelligence comes from humility, listening, and absorbing new ideas. Kiyosaki highlights that many educated people fail financially because they ignore practical learning. Investing in the mind is more important than buying real estate or stocks, as knowledge creates long-term wealth and opportunities.

Stay tuned to discover the next steps to unlocking your full potential!

title: Chapter 8: Getting Started - Part 1
excerpt: Insights on Kiyosaki's Cashflow Quadrant and wealth building: Chapter 8: Getting Started - Part 1
content:
Ready to jump in? Let’s explore the next chapter: Getting Started!

As the title suggests, this chapter is all about laying the foundation for our financial success. Here, Robert Kiyosaki shares 10 steps to awaken our financial genius. Wondering what these steps are and how they can help? Let’s find out!

Kiyosaki admits that getting rich wasn’t easy for him. When people ask, “How do I start?” he shares the approach he uses every day. Finding good financial opportunities isn’t that hard, it’s like learning to ride a bike. At first, it’s wobbly, but with practice and determination, it becomes natural. That determination is a personal challenge that everyone must face.

To find life-changing opportunities, Kiyosaki says we need to awaken our financial genius, a special ability we all have. Many people don’t realize this because society teaches us to work for money, not make money work for us. Schools focus on professions, not financial freedom. Most people trust their job or the government to keep them safe, but this leaves their children unprepared for the future. The common message we get is: work hard, earn money, spend it, and borrow more when it’s gone. Kiyosaki says real financial freedom comes from thinking differently and developing your own money skills.

He also notes that 90% of people follow the regular path - get a job, earn a paycheck - because it’s easier. But for those willing to think differently, there’s another way. Kiyosaki shares 10 steps he personally follows to unlock your financial genius. You can use his steps or create your own, your genius is unique.

While in Peru, he talked to a gold miner with 45 years of experience. When asked how he always found gold, the miner said, “There is gold everywhere. Most people are not trained to see it.” Kiyosaki says the same idea applies to money. For example, in real estate, he can spot four or five great deals in a day - while most people find nothing, even in the same area. The difference is knowing how to see opportunities - developing your financial genius.

These 10 steps are meant to help you unlock your money skills and take control of your financial future.

Want to learn the steps? Stay tuned!

title: Chapter 7: Overcoming Obstacles - Part 4
excerpt: Insights on Kiyosaki's Cashflow Quadrant and wealth building: Chapter 7: Overcoming Obstacles - Part 4
content:
Ready to learn how overcoming bad habits and arrogance can make your life more productive?

Robert Kiyosaki explains that people’s lives are shaped more by their habits than by their education. He uses the example of Arnold Schwarzenegger. Many people admire Arnold’s body, but instead of copying his habit of working out every day, they make excuses - saying he was born that way or changing the subject. This shows how habits control behavior.

Kiyosaki then shares a lesson he learned from his “rich dad” about money habits. Rich dad asked when Kiyosaki’s poor dad paid his bills. The answer was: at the beginning of the month. When asked if any money was left over, the answer was: very little. Rich dad said this was the core problem - poor dad paid everyone else first and himself last.

Rich dad explained that he also pays his bills on time, but he pays himself first, even before the government. When asked what happens if there isn’t enough money, rich dad said he still pays himself first. The reason is not “how” he does it, but why.

The “why” is motivation. If rich dad doesn’t pay his creditors, they complain loudly. If he doesn’t pay himself, no one complains. By paying himself first, he creates pressure to find more income. That pressure pushes him to work harder, think smarter, start businesses, invest, and look for opportunities. If he paid himself last, there would be no pressure - and he would stay broke.

Rich dad compares bill collectors and tax authorities to bullies. Most people give in to these bullies and always pay them first, while neglecting themselves. As a result, they remain financially weak. Rich dad chose a different path: he used the fear of these bullies as motivation to grow stronger, just like lifting heavier weights builds muscle.

By paying himself first, rich dad strengthened his financial thinking and discipline. By paying himself last, people become weaker and allow bosses, landlords, and bill collectors to control their lives. According to Kiyosaki, good money habits make people strong, while bad money habits keep them powerless, just like the “98 - pound weakling.”

Robert Kiyosaki explains that arrogance is expensive. He believes that what a person knows can make them money, but what they don’t know can cost them money. Every time he acted arrogant, he lost money - because arrogance made him think that the things he didn’t understand were unimportant.

He points out that many people use arrogance to hide their ignorance, especially in finance. He often sees this when talking with accountants or investors who speak confidently but don’t actually understand financial statements. They are not exactly lying, but they are also not being truthful - they are just talking their way through the conversation.

Kiyosaki says many people in the money and investment world don’t truly understand what they are talking about. Instead, they repeat sales pitches, much like used-car salesmen. His advice is simple, when you realize you don’t know something, don’t pretend. Admit it, and then educate yourself by learning from experts or reading good books.

Stay tuned to see what the next chapter holds for you!

title: Chapter 7: Overcoming Obstacles - Part 3
excerpt: Insights on Kiyosaki's Cashflow Quadrant and wealth building: Chapter 7: Overcoming Obstacles - Part 3
content:
The next reason smart people fail is laziness!

Robert Kiyosaki explains that many people who seem busy are actually avoiding important responsibilities. For example, a businessman may work long hours to provide for his family but ignore problems at home. He stays busy at the office to avoid dealing with his marriage. One day, his wife leaves with the children, and his work suffers - he eventually loses his job too.

Kiyosaki says this hidden laziness is common. People stay “busy” to avoid thinking about money, health, or other important issues. Even outside work, they distract themselves with TV, shopping, hobbies, or sports. This is laziness - not doing nothing, but avoiding what really matters.

So how do we overcome it? Kiyosaki suggests a little greed - not just for money, but for a better life, better health, and freedom. This desire pushes people to face uncomfortable truths and take action.

Many people grow up being told that wanting more is bad. Parents might say, “That’s selfish” or “We can’t afford it”. This creates guilt and fear around money. Some parents suppress desire; others overspend out of guilt. Either way, children often grow up unprepared for the future.

Kiyosaki’s “rich dad” taught the opposite. Instead of saying “I can’t afford it,” he encouraged asking: “How can I afford it?” This simple question trains the mind to think creatively, explore options, and find solutions. It helps people believe that anything is possible.

When people say “I can’t afford it” repeatedly, a conflict arises. The spirit wants growth and improvement, but the lazy mind wants comfort and safety. Excuses like “I’m too tired” or “It’s risky” take over, and progress stops. This can lead to sadness or even depression.

On the other hand, asking “How can I afford it?” opens the mind to opportunities and ideas. Rich dad focused on teaching his children how to think, not just giving them money. Learning to solve problems builds skills and confidence - more valuable than money itself.

Kiyosaki believes that wanting a better life is natural and healthy. Laziness is overcome by asking: “What’s in it for me?” Imagining freedom, security, and choice motivates action. Progress happens because people want something better - better lives, better tools, better futures.

A little greed is good. Too much can be harmful, but guilt is worse - it kills ambition. Wanting more isn’t wrong. People will always be criticized, so the key is to do what feels right for you. Healthy self-interest fuels growth, learning, and success.

Stay tuned to learn how to achieve success beyond bad habits and arrogance!

title: Chapter 7: Overcoming Obstacles - Part 2
excerpt: Insights on Kiyosaki's Cashflow Quadrant and wealth building: Chapter 7: Overcoming Obstacles - Part 2
content:
So, what’s this week holding for you? Let’s dive in!

This week’s topic: overcoming cynicism.

Robert Kiyosaki says that inside all of us lives a little “Chicken Little” - that voice that panics and imagines the worst. We all get stuck in doubt sometimes. Thoughts like, “I’m not smart enough” or “I can’t do this” sneak in and make us hesitate. Then we play the “What if?” game: “What if I lose money?” “What if it all goes wrong?” And sometimes, the people around us make it worse with comments like, “That will never work” or “Why would you even try that?”

When fear and doubt get too loud, we freeze. We stay safe, miss opportunities, and watch life pass us by. Kiyosaki calls all this fear-driven chatter “noise.” This idea comes from investor Peter Lynch, who noticed that in the 1950s, fear of nuclear war made people spend on fallout shelters instead of investing. If they had invested, many might have become financially independent. Kiyosaki uses this story to show that fear often stops people from taking the right actions, and the trick is learning to see past the noise and act wisely.

When bad news hits, like violence, food scares, or illness - people panic. Guns fly off the shelves, restaurants overreact, and cold medicine disappears. Most people let fear control their decisions. They act like “Chicken Littles,” running around screaming, “The sky is falling!” Even smart people feel that little panic inside.

But smart investors? They see opportunity in fear. When everyone else is too scared to move, that’s when the real money is made.

Kiyosaki shares the story of his friend Richard. In Phoenix, property prices were low. Kiyosaki showed him a bargain townhome that could generate cash flow and grow in value. Richard was ready to buy, but he canceled after listening to a neighbor who wasn’t an investor. Fear and doubt, classic buyer’s remorse - stopped him. Years later, the property’s value and income had skyrocketed. Richard missed a chance at financial freedom.

Kiyosaki also talks about safer, conventional investments like CDs - but he sometimes puts a small portion of his money into tax-lien certificates. These earn about 16% a year, are backed by real estate, and enforced by state law. They’re secure, though not very liquid, so he treats them like 2- to 7-year CDs.

When he tells people about this, many immediately call it risky. But their doubts usually come from secondhand advice, from friends or magazines - not from personal experience. They settle for lower returns because they’re afraid. And as Kiyosaki says, doubt is expensive.

His big point: fear and cynicism keep most people poor. The world is full of opportunities - but doubt stops people from seeing them. His “rich dad” always said, “Cynics never win. Unchecked doubt and fear create cynics.” Critics blind people, but analysis opens eyes. Winners don’t just complain - they study, spot opportunities others miss, and act. Seeing what others overlook? That’s the key to success.

And guess what? We’re just getting started. Stay tuned - you won’t want to miss what comes next!

title: Chapter 7: Overcoming Obstacles - Part 1
excerpt: Insights on Kiyosaki's Cashflow Quadrant and wealth building: Chapter 7: Overcoming Obstacles - Part 1
content:
Now we are in Chapter 7, titled “Overcoming Obstacles.” This chapter takes us into the challenges and roadblocks that keep people from achieving financial independence, and shows how the right mindset can turn those obstacles into opportunities.

Even after learning about money, we can still struggle to build wealth. Robert Kiyosaki points out five main reasons why smart people fail: fear, cynicism, laziness, bad habits, and arrogance. Of these, fear - especially fear of losing money, is the biggest problem.

Kiyosaki explains that everyone fears losing money, even rich people. The difference is in how we handle that fear. Poor people avoid risk and play it safe, often missing opportunities. Rich people take calculated risks and use failures as lessons.

He gives the example of Texans, who take bold risks and learn from their mistakes. “If you’re going to go broke, go big,” they say. Similarly, Fran Tarkenton, a famous NFL quarterback, believes that winning means not being afraid to lose. Kiyosaki reminds us that losing is part of learning - whether it’s riding a bike, playing golf, or investing. For winners, failure motivates improvement; for losers, it discourages them.

Many people play it safe financially, investing in low-risk, balanced portfolios. While this feels secure, it usually doesn’t make us rich. Real success comes from focus: putting our energy and money into fewer, bigger opportunities instead of spreading ourselves too thin.

Kiyosaki also stresses the importance of starting early. The sooner we start investing, the more time we have to learn from mistakes and grow our wealth. If fear makes us too cautious, it’s okay to play it safe - but we should remember that financial freedom takes courage, patience, and the ability to learn from failure.

In short, building wealth is simple math-wise, but attitude is what separates winners from losers. Winners learn from failure, while losers avoid it. Remember the Alamo: even big defeats can inspire great success.

Up next, we’ll uncover more secrets together. Don’t miss it!

title: Chapter 6: Work To Learn - Don’t Work For Money - Part 4
excerpt: Insights on Kiyosaki's Cashflow Quadrant and wealth building: Chapter 6: Work To Learn - Don’t Work For Money - Part 4
content:
Ready to see the final lesson - from “ Work To Learn - Don’t Work For Money” ?

Kiyosaki explains that his “poor dad” believed the key to success was becoming very specialized. The more educated and skilled someone was in one area, the more money they thought they would earn. Even after losing his government job, his father still believed in focusing on one skill and supported teachers’ unions to protect specialized workers. Kiyosaki often disagreed, but his father didn’t see that being too specialized can actually trap people in one skill, making them dependent on others for security.

His “rich dad” taught the opposite. He believed it was better to learn many skills instead of just one. He encouraged Kiyosaki and his friend to understand all parts of a business, not just one role. Many successful companies do this by rotating employees through different jobs so they see the big picture. Wealthy families often do the same with their children, helping them understand how businesses really work.

Kiyosaki points out that in the past, staying in one job for life was considered smart. Today, changing jobs and learning new skills is smarter. Focusing on learning skills rather than just chasing a bigger paycheck may earn less at first, but it leads to more freedom and bigger rewards in the long run.

The most important skills are sales and marketing.

By sales, Kiyosaki doesn’t just mean selling products, he means communicating effectively with anyone: customers, coworkers, bosses, family, or friends. Skills like writing, speaking, and negotiating are essential, and he constantly practices and learns to get better at them.

He explains that his educated dad became very skilled in one job, but that also limited his choices. When he lost his government job, he realized how vulnerable he was because he only had one skill. Kiyosaki compares this to athletes who earn a lot but suddenly lose everything when they get injured or grow too old.

His rich dad, on the other hand, encouraged him to learn a little about many things and to work with people who were smarter. Together, they could combine talents and create better results. Today, this is called teamwork or synergy.

Many people earn a lot not because they are experts in one thing, but because they combine skills. For example, some teachers now earn high incomes because they can teach, sell, and market. Kiyosaki believes selling and marketing are the most valuable skills. Many people struggle with them because they fear rejection, but learning to communicate and handle rejection makes life much easier.

Being highly technical can also be a weakness. Some people are geniuses but cannot communicate, so they earn very little. Kiyosaki advises them to spend a year learning to sell, even if they earn nothing at first - because strong communication skills are priceless.

Giving and receiving is key to wealth.

Kiyosaki says that to be truly rich, you must be both a good student and a good teacher. Many people stay poor because they are unwilling to learn or share what they know.
