Both of his fathers believed in giving first. Teaching was one way they gave, and his rich dad also gave money to churches, charities, and foundations. He knew that giving money attracts more money. This is why wealthy families often create foundations that both grow and give away wealth over time.

His educated dad believed he would give only after he had extra money, but that extra never came. Instead of giving first, he focused on working harder to earn more. Kiyosaki says this is backwardsthe real rule of money is: give first, and you will receive.

In the end, Kiyosaki became a mix of both fathers. He is a capitalist who loves making money, but also a teacher who cares about the growing gap between the rich and the poor. He believes the outdated education system is one of the main reasons this gap keeps widening.

And stay tuned - an even more interesting chapter is coming next!

title: Chapter 6: Work To Learn - Don’t Work For Money - Part 3
excerpt: Insights on Kiyosaki's Cashflow Quadrant and wealth building: Chapter 6: Work To Learn - Don’t Work For Money - Part 3
content:
In Jerry Maguire, there’s a famous line: “Show me the money.” But the line Kiyosaki finds most revealing comes when Tom Cruise leaves his firm and asks, “Who wants to come with me?” The whole office is silent, except for one woman who says, “I’d like to, but I’m due for a promotion in three months.”

Kiyosaki uses this to illustrate how most people live their lives - chasing pay raises, promotions, and security, always busy, but rarely asking, “Where is this actually taking me?” People work hard, go back to school, earn more qualifications, and then face the same disappointments, never truly planning for their future.

He highlights the harsh reality of retirement. In The Retirement Myth, Craig Karpel describes a pension consultant who sarcastically calls the lack of retirement savings for most people “The Silver Bullet” - implying that many may face drastic measures if they cannot afford to live in old age. Traditional pensions are disappearing, 401(k) plans put the risk on workers, and medical expenses combined with long-term care make the outlook even more alarming.

Kiyosaki warns that most people focus only on their next paycheck, not their long-term future. He encourages thinking ahead. Instead of working solely for money and security, he suggests learning new skills - such as sales, through a second job or network marketing. These experiences teach resilience, how to handle failure, and the kind of education that can be more valuable than money over the long term.

When people resist, saying it’s too much hassle or not interesting, Kiyosaki reminds them: we do things like exercise for long-term benefits, even if we don’t enjoy them. Similarly, taking control of your skills and future is worth the effort.

He often points out that it’s hard for people to change if they aren’t used to it, like the old saying, “You can’t teach an old dog new tricks.” But he encourages people to push themselves to learn new things, comparing it to going to the gym: the hardest part is deciding to start, but once you do, it becomes easier and even enjoyable.

Kiyosaki explains that if someone chooses to specialize deeply in one field, it’s wise to ensure their work is protected by a union. His dad, for example, became head of the teachers’ union in Hawaii and said it was one of the toughest jobs he ever held. His rich dad, on the other hand, worked to keep his businesses free from unions. Kiyosaki sees the value in both approaches.

He emphasizes that highly specialized skills don’t always transfer easily to other industries. For example, a senior airline pilot or a doctor might struggle to move into a completely different field because their skills are so specific. That’s why unions can be critical, they protect people whose skills are valuable in only one area. His rule: specialize first, then unionize if needed.

Kiyosaki also stresses the importance of learning business, not just a skill. Many talented people fail because they focus on perfecting their craft but don’t understand business systems. He tells the story of an artist in Hawaii who inherited money and tried to grow his business with advertising but failed because he didn’t understand marketing or sales. Even though he was talented, he didn’t know how to run a business, and he refused to learn.

Want to learn more about Robert Kiyosaki’s insights and the key management skills he recommends? Stay tuned!

title: Chapter 6: Work To Learn - Don’t Work For Money - Part 2
excerpt: Insights on Kiyosaki's Cashflow Quadrant and wealth building: Chapter 6: Work To Learn - Don’t Work For Money - Part 2
content:
Ready to uncover more useful insights?

When Robert Kiyosaki published his first book, If You Want To Be Rich and Happy, Don’t Go to School, a publisher suggested a more academic title: The Economics of Education. Robert joked that with a title like that, he would only sell two copies - one to his family and one to his friend, and even they would want it for free.

He kept the bold and controversial title because he knew it would attract attention and get media exposure. People thought he was crazy, but the strategy worked, the book sold extremely well. He later explained that he wasn’t against education; instead, he believed the system needed serious reform. His bold title was simply a way to start that conversation.

After graduating from the U.S. Merchant Marine Academy in 1969, Robert secured a well-respected job at Standard Oil, earning about $42,000 a year while working only seven months. His academic father was proud. But after just six months, Robert quit to join the Marine Corps and become a pilot. His educated dad was shocked and disappointed - his rich dad, however, congratulated him.

Robert points out that society encourages specialization - doctors, lawyers, and professionals are trained to master one field. His educated dad believed in that approach and was proud of earning his PhD. But his rich dad believed the opposite. He taught Robert: “You want to know a little about a lot.”

So Robert spent years working across different departments in his rich dad’s businesses - accounting, sales, marketing, construction, and even cleaning tables. His rich dad wanted him to learn the language and mindset of business, not just one role. He also had Robert sit in meetings with bankers, lawyers, accountants, and brokers to understand how an entire business functions, not just one part of it.

Robert explains that when he quit his high-paying job at Standard Oil, his educated dad couldn’t understand why. To him, a stable job with benefits and promotion opportunities was the smartest path. But Robert wasn’t following his dad’s logic, he was following his rich dad’s mindset.

To his educated dad, job security was everything. To his rich dad, learning was everything.

Robert didn’t attend school just to become a ship’s officer, he went to study international trade. While others partied, he traveled across Asia and the Pacific, learning about business, culture, and global markets.

Later, Robert joined the Marine Corps, not just to fly, but to develop leadership skills. His rich dad believed that leading people was the hardest, and most valuable - skill in business.

After returning from Vietnam, Robert joined Xerox, not for the salary, but to overcome his fear of selling. His educated dad looked down on sales, but his rich dad encouraged it because sales and communication are core business skills. After becoming a top performer, Robert quit again to continue learning and growing.

At age 30, he finally started his first company. His rich dad believed it was better to fail young while there was still time to recover. Robert’s first product, a nylon-and-Velcro wallet - was manufactured overseas and shipped to the U.S., testing everything he had learned.

Robert continues doing international business and investing globally, especially in emerging markets, just as his rich dad taught him.

He often shares a saying: “JOB stands for Just Over Broke.”

Most people work just enough to pay bills, staying trapped in the Rat Race. They work hard enough not to get fired, and companies pay just enough so employees don’t quit. Many never get ahead because they focus only on earning money - not on learning skills.

Robert advises young people to choose jobs based on what they will learn, not what they will earn. Otherwise, life becomes like a hamster wheel - lots of movement, but no real progress.

He reminds us to stop and ask: “Where is all this hard work taking you?”

If the answer leads nowhere, then something needs to change - right?

So, what’s your thought on this?

title: Chapter 6: Work To Learn - Don’t Work For Money - Part 1
excerpt: Insights on Kiyosaki's Cashflow Quadrant and wealth building: Chapter 6: Work To Learn - Don’t Work For Money - Part 1
content:
Ready to delve into what this chapter holds for us?

He begins this chapter by sharing a thought-provoking story - curious to know what it is?

A few years ago, Robert Kiyosaki gave an interview to a newspaper in Singapore. The young female reporter was punctual, and they began talking right away over coffee in a fancy hotel lobby. Robert was there to share the stage with Zig Ziglar - Zig on motivation, and Robert on The Secrets of the Rich.

During their chat, the reporter mentioned that she dreamed of becoming a best-selling author.

Robert had read her articles and was impressed - they were clear, strong, and engaging. He asked her what was holding her back. She admitted that, although people praised her novels, nothing ever seemed to happen. So she stuck with her newspaper job because it paid the bills.

Robert encouraged her, suggesting a practical step: attending a sales-training course run by a friend in Singapore who trained people for top corporations. He believed this could give her the skills to advance her career and finally achieve her dreams.

She was shocked and offended. To her, studying sales seemed beneath her because she had a master’s degree in English Literature and saw herself as a professional. She disliked salespeople and thought all they cared about was money.

Kiyosaki gently pushed back. He showed her her own notes, pointing out that while he was a “bestselling author,” she could be a “best-writing author” if she combined her writing talent with sales skills. He emphasized that education and talent alone weren’t enough to succeed in the real world - you also needed to know how to sell.

She left angry, refusing to “stoop so low” as to learn sales. Still, she acknowledged him professionally in her write-up. Kiyosaki concluded that the world is full of smart, talented, and educated people, but success often comes from combining talent with practical skills, like sales, which allow you to turn your abilities into real results.

He noticed that many talented people earn far less than they should. Brilliant, highly educated professionals - doctors, dentists, even writers, often struggle financially. It’s not a lack of talent, but the absence of one key skill. As one consultant said, “They are one skill away from great wealth.”

Most people only know how to work hard. But financial intelligence comes from combining skills: accounting, investing, marketing, and law. Learning just one additional skill, like sales or public relations - can dramatically increase income.

Take the young newspaper writer. If she mastered sales, marketing, advertising, and PR, she could turn her talent into real results. She could learn how to promote her writing, attract attention, and eventually sell her novel successfully. In short, education and talent alone aren’t enough - combining them with practical skills is the true path to financial success.

Just stay tuned - there’s more to come!

title: Chapter 5: The Rich Invent Money - Part 6
excerpt: Insights on Kiyosaki's Cashflow Quadrant and wealth building: Chapter 5: The Rich Invent Money - Part 6
content:
Ready to explore the final insights from The Rich Invent Money?

At first, he bought a small house in Oregon for a low price during a weak market. A year later, when California investors started buying property in Oregon, he sold the house for $95,000, making about $40,000 in profit. Instead of paying taxes on that gain right away, he used a 1031 tax-deferred exchange, which allowed him to reinvest the money without paying taxes immediately.

He then bought a 12-unit apartment building near Intel in Beaverton, Oregon. The sellers lived in Germany and didn’t know the real value of the property, so he bought it for $300,000 - far below market price. Two years later, he sold it for $495,000 and used another 1031 exchange to buy a 30- unit apartment building in Phoenix, Arizona.

That Phoenix property produced more than $5,000 a month in rental income. A few years later, as the market improved, a buyer offered $1.2 million for it.

Robert uses this story to make one clear point: Even a small investment can grow into a large one if you understand money, markets, and the laws that support investing.

He also emphasizes that people who don’t learn about finance tend to follow traditional advice like “play it safe” and “diversify.” While these “secure” investments feel safe, they often produce very low returns because they are designed to minimize risk, and therefore minimize profit.

Most big brokerage firms avoid risky deals to protect themselves and their clients. Because of that, beginners rarely get access to the “hot” investments that make the wealthy even richer. These opportunities usually go to people who already understand how money and investing work.

That’s why developing financial intelligence over time is so important. The smarter you become with money, the more opportunities you recognize, and the easier it is to judge whether a deal is good or bad. Many people stay “safe,” work hard, but never grow financially because they don’t build this knowledge.

Robert explains that he grows wealth by planting small “financial seeds.” He starts with small investments, $5,000 or $10,000 - that grow over time into million-dollar assets through rising markets and smart trading. His real estate portfolio and stock investments began this way.

He also invests in high-risk private companies before they go public. These small early investments can grow extremely fast, sometimes turning $25,000 into $1 million within a year - when the company succeeds.

He explains that investing is not gambling when you actually understand what you’re doing. It becomes gambling only when you put money into something blindly and hope for the best. With the right knowledge, experience, and financial intelligence, you can reduce the risk, even though risk will always exist.

Robert emphasizes that people should invest more in financial education than in any investment product. What seems risky to one person may be safer for someone who has the right skills and understanding.

He also shares that some of his own investments are very high-risk for most people, but he has been learning and practicing since 1979. With enough knowledge and the right setup, things that look risky to others can become manageable, and even highly profitable.

Nothing he shares is meant as advice; they are just examples of what is possible. He believes that an average person can build over $100,000 in passive income within 5 - 10 years if they stay smart and keep expenses modest.

His foundation is real estate, because it is stable, slow-moving, and provides steady cash flow. Having this strong, safe base allows him to take bigger risks in other areas, like speculative stocks.

Robert explains that after earning profits in the stock market, he reinvests the money into real estate to build a strong financial foundation. He emphasizes that good property deals exist everywhere, even in expensive cities like New York, Tokyo, and Singapore - but most people miss them because they don’t know how to look.

He says that great opportunities are found with your mind, not your eyes. Many people never become wealthy because they aren’t financially trained to recognize opportunities right in front of them

Robert counsels novices not to worry about their mistakes. It is similar to when you learned to walk or ride a bike, you fell and got back up. Most people do not get rich because they are too afraid of losing.

He also describes two types of investors; those who purchase investments that have been developed and structured, such as stocks, mutual funds, or real estate deals, and those who put different opportunities together to create their own investments - that is the more sophisticated and lucrative type.

You need to develop some skills in order to become that second type of investor.

1. Spot Opportunities Others Overlook

Some people can see value where others see nothing.

For example, someone bought an ugly, rundown house. Everyone thought he was crazy - until he discovered it came with four empty lots. He demolished the house and sold all five lots for three times the price he paid. Just by noticing what others missed, he made $75,000 in two months.

2. Learn How to Raise Money

Most people think they need a bank to invest. Smart investors know how to raise money in different ways. Many great deals can be done with little or no personal cash if you know how to structure them. It’s not about having money, it’s about knowing how to find it. Robert even tied up a $1.2M property with no money, then sold the deal for a quick $50,000 profit in just three days.

3. Work With Smarter People

Successful investors surround themselves with experts. Good advice matters. Your knowledge is your real wealth, and what you don’t know can be your biggest risk. Instead of avoiding risk, learn how to manage it.

Stay tuned - there’s more to explore in the next chapter!

title: Chapter 5: The Rich Invent Money - Part 5
excerpt: Insights on Kiyosaki's Cashflow Quadrant and wealth building: Chapter 5: The Rich Invent Money - Part 5
content:
Ready to explore the four main skills that build financial intelligence? Let’s break them down together.

1. Accounting - This is the language of money. When we can read financial numbers, we can understand how a business or investment is really performing.

2. Investing - Investing is simply using our money to create more money. We put money into something that grows, and over time, it pays us back even more.

3. Understanding Markets - Markets run on supply and demand - what people want and what’s available. When we understand this, we start spotting opportunities and good deals that others might overlook.

4. The Law - Every business and investment has rules. When we know those rules, we make smarter decisions and avoid unnecessary problems.

When we combine these four skills, we build a strong foundation for handling money. With that foundation, we can make money in many ways - buying houses, investing in stocks, starting businesses, collecting valuable assets, and more.

Robert often reminds us that markets never stay the same. When real estate and the stock market were booming and everyone was rushing in, he noticed the shift and quietly sold some investments. He simply paid attention, watched his assets grow, planned ahead, and worked with his accountant to manage the cash wisely.

The bigger lesson he teaches is that opportunities are always around us. Most people miss them because they don’t know how to see them. But as the world and technology keep changing, even more opportunities appear. That’s why building our financial intelligence matters so much.

He believes that when the economy changes, some people panic and lose their jobs, while others use those same changes to get ahead. What separates the two groups isn’t luck - it’s financial intelligence. It’s the ability to turn life’s “lemons” into something valuable.

He shares his investment stories not to brag, but to show how simple skills can turn ordinary situations into wealth. He knows some may think he’s showing off, but that’s not the point. He wants us to see that building wealth isn’t complicated once we understand the basics.

For himself, he focuses mainly on two things: real estate and small-cap stocks.

Real estate gives him steady, reliable cash flow. Small-cap stocks are where he looks for faster growth. But he never expects us to copy him. If something doesn’t make sense to him, he simply avoids it. For him, building wealth is mostly about simple math, common sense, and a willingness to learn.

One story he loves to share goes back to 1989. He used to jog through a quiet, charming neighborhood in Portland, filled with tiny, fairy-tale houses. But the economy was terrible, the timber industry had collapsed, the stock market had crashed, and “For Sale” signs were everywhere.

One house had been for sale far longer than the rest. One day, he met the tired-looking owner outside. When he asked the price, the owner practically begged him to make an offer, nobody had looked at the house for months.

He took a quick tour, liked what he saw, and bought it within 30 minutes for $20,000 less than the asking price. It was a small 1930s home with a stone fireplace, perfect as a rental.

He put $5,000 down on a $45,000 house worth around $65,000, simply because the market was depressed and nobody else wanted it. The seller moved out relieved, and a college professor moved in right away as the first tenant.

After paying the mortgage, expenses, and management fees, he made just under $40 a month. Not a huge amount, but it was a safe, steady beginning. And it showed how simple opportunities appear when we train ourselves to recognize them.

And there’s still one more valuable insight ahead - stay tuned!

title: Chapter 5: The Rich Invent Money - Part 4
excerpt: Insights on Kiyosaki's Cashflow Quadrant and wealth building: Chapter 5: The Rich Invent Money - Part 4
content:
In this section, Robert Kiyosaki explains how leveraging money intelligently can produce far greater returns than traditional saving.

Yes, he shares an example of a second real estate transaction in which, after reselling a property, he could have pocketed $2,000 and re-extended the loan for another 30 years. He reflects on the power of earning money from money, questioning what the true ROI would be if one gets paid to make money in the first place.

Robert contrasts this approach with the conventional idea of saving $100 a month for 40 years, emphasizing how taxes and low interest rates erode real gains. To him, that method might be safe, but it’s far from smart.

As the Phoenix real estate market grew stronger, Robert notes that the houses once sold for $60,000 eventually rose to $110,000. While foreclosure opportunities still existed, they became scarce and time-consuming to find. Recognizing that time itself is a valuable asset, he shifted his focus to new opportunities that could go into his “asset column.”

Kiyosaki also highlights the skepticism he often encounters - people saying, “You can’t do that here” or “That’s against the law,” instead of asking how it’s done. He reminds readers that the math is simple, and professionals like escrow companies handle the legal and payment details.

He enjoys the benefits of investing without the hassles of property maintenance, since the buyers are responsible for their own homes. Even when payments fall through, he sees it as another opportunity - through late fees or resale, all managed by the court system.

Robert emphasizes that while this strategy might not work everywhere due to different market conditions, it clearly shows how a simple financial process can generate hundreds of thousands of dollars with minimal money and low risk. He points out that money is essentially just an agreement, and anyone with a basic education can understand and apply this method.

Despite this, most people stick to traditional advice like “work hard and save money,” missing opportunities to grow real wealth. In his example, about 30 hours of effort resulted in roughly $190,000 added to the asset column, and without paying taxes.

Robert reminds readers that time is one of their most valuable assets. He points out that if someone chooses the traditional route of saving money, they should consider how long it would take to save $190,000 - emphasizing that time matters just as much as money.

He also questions the common belief that a good education alone guarantees success. While academic achievements are valuable, Robert argues they may not be enough to build real wealth.

Using a small investment example, he shows how modest actions can grow into significant gains. His overall point is that a strong financial foundation, built on solid financial education - is essential for long-term success.

There are four essential skills behind financial intelligence. Ready to discover them? Stay with us!

title: Chapter 5: The Rich Invent Money - Part 3
excerpt: Insights on Kiyosaki's Cashflow Quadrant and wealth building: Chapter 5: The Rich Invent Money - Part 3
content:
Ok, all set to uncover what this week’s insights hold?

The main idea is that financial intelligence means having many ways to solve money problems and create opportunities - not just working hard and saving.

Most people know only one way to deal with money: work, save, and borrow. But people with high financial intelligence can think creatively, find different solutions, and even turn bad situations into opportunities.

Robert explains that:

• You can’t just wait for the “right time” or “luck.” You create your own luck through knowledge and smart thinking 

• Money isn’t real, it’s something people agree has value. What really matters is how you use your mind to create wealth 

• In today’s world, people can make money from ideas, agreements, and smart decisions, not just from physical work.

He gives an example:

During a bad economy in Phoenix, instead of saving money, he invested in cheap houses that others were selling.

He used creativity and quick thinking - borrowing a small amount from a friend, buying a property cheaply, and selling it fast for a profit.

In just a few hours, he made tens of thousands of dollars, showing how financial intelligence can create money from opportunities others miss.

Yes, House prices had dropped, homes that used to cost $100,000 were now $75,000. Instead of buying through normal real estate agents, he looked for bargain deals at bankruptcy offices and courthouse sales, where he could buy those same houses for as little as $20,000.

He borrowed $2,000 from a friend for 90 days (and agreed to pay back $200 extra as interest) to use as a down payment. While the house purchase was still being processed, he advertised the same house for $60,000 with no money down to attract buyers.

Many people showed interest, and once he officially owned the house, it sold very quickly. The buyer paid him a $2,500 processing fee, and the legal companies handled the rest. He repaid his friend $2,200, keeping everyone happy - his friend, the lawyer, the buyer, and himself.

In the end, he sold a $20,000 house for $60,000, making $40,000 in profit, mostly through smart timing and creative financing - all in about five hours of work.

Since we’re getting better at understanding money and numbers, Robert shows us how this example proves that money was actually invented by people.

During a slow housing market, Kim and Robert used their spare time to complete six small real estate deals.

These simple “buy, create, and sell” transactions earned them about $190,000 in assets (notes at 10 percent interest) and $19,000 a year in income.

By managing the money through their private company, they could use much of it for businessrelated expenses like travel, cars, and client meetings - legally reducing their taxes.

This is a clear example of how money can be invented, created, and protected using financial intelligence.

Robert asks us to think about how long it would take to save $190,000, and whether a bank would ever pay 10% interest on that money.

He explains that his promissory note (a loan agreement) lasts for 30 years, earning him $19,000 a year, which adds up to over $500,000 in total income. He even says he hopes not to be paid back the full $190,000, because if he is, he’d have to pay taxes on it.

If the buyer stops paying, it’s still not a big problem. He can take back the house and sell it again, maybe for $70,000, and collect another $2,500 fee. The new buyer would still see it as a no-moneydown deal, and the process could continue again.

So, what are your thoughts on this? Just think about it - and let’s meet again for more useful insights next time.

title: Chapter 5: The Rich Invent Money - Part 2
excerpt: Insights on Kiyosaki's Cashflow Quadrant and wealth building: Chapter 5: The Rich Invent Money - Part 2
content:
Are you ready to uncover what Robert is trying to teach us?

According to Robert, he focuses on improving his financial knowledge because this is the most exciting time to be alive. He prefers welcoming change rather than fearing it and is more interested in creating wealth than worrying about a paycheck.

Robert points out that the current era is unprecedented in history, future generations will look back and see it as a period of both turmoil and opportunity, where the old ways are ending and the new are emerging. He emphasizes that developing financial IQ allows one to prosper in this dynamic environment, while ignoring it may lead to being left behind as others move boldly forward.

He also explains how the nature of wealth has changed over time:

• 300 years ago, wealth was in land; whoever owned the land was wealthy.

• Later, wealth shifted to factories and production, leading to the rise of industrialists.

• Today, wealth lies in information, and those with the most timely knowledge gain the advantage.

Robert highlights that in today’s world, information moves at the speed of light, making wealth borderless and fast-moving. He predicts a dramatic rise in new multimillionaires, while some will inevitably be left behind. His core message is that financial intelligence combined with courage and timely action is essential to thrive in this era.

He also emphasizes how clinging to old ideas can hold people back financially. Many struggle not because of the economy, technology, or their boss, but because they resist change. Old ideas that once helped can become liabilities when they are no longer relevant.

Robert shares a story from one of his CASHFLOW® classes, a board game he created to teach people how money works. A recently divorced woman attended, hoping to learn about investing. At f irst, she was excited when she got a “boat” card in the game, but as she tried to understand the numbers—income statements, balance sheets, and cash flow - she became frustrated. She didn’t like math, and the game began to feel overwhelming. Later, she faced life challenges, including being downsized and having a child, making the game even harder for her.

Despite her frustration, Robert emphasizes that the game is designed to reflect real-life financial behavior. It teaches how monthly cash flow from assets must exceed expenses to escape the “Rat Race” and move onto the “Fast Track.” The game provides instant feedback, showing participants what they know, what they need to learn, and how their behavior affects their finances.

Robert notes that many people resist this reflection. The woman even demanded her money back, believing the game couldn’t possibly mirror her life. Yet over time, she began to see connections: she and her ex-husband had accumulated many “doodads” but few real assets, and her lack of f inancial understanding during the marriage left her feeling powerless. Her frustration came not just from the game, but from realizing she hadn’t taken control of her finances.

Since 1984, Robert has successfully used games and simulations to teach financial literacy. He believes that unlike traditional lectures, games reflect behavior and provide personalized lessons, helping students see their strengths, weaknesses, and financial blind spots. This approach allows people to learn actively and prepares them to make smarter decisions with their money.

Understanding money isn’t just about knowledge - it’s about behavior, awareness, and taking responsibility. Many fail financially because they ignore this, but those who embrace change and learn from feedback can prosper.

To learn more useful insights on investments, just stay tuned !

title: Chapter 5: The Rich Invent Money - Part 1
excerpt: Insights on Kiyosaki's Cashflow Quadrant and wealth building: Chapter 5: The Rich Invent Money - Part 1
content:
Often in the real world, it’s not the smartest people who succeed, but the boldest.

Robert begins this chapter with this powerful statement. In “The Rich Invent Money,” he explains why courage and action matter more than just knowledge.

He tells a story about Alexander Graham Bell. Bell had just invented the telephone and was struggling to meet the high demand. He offered to sell his patent and small company to Western Union for $100,000, but the company refused. Despite this, Bell’s invention eventually led to a multi-billion-dollar industry and the creation of AT&T.

Robert also recalls a news story about a local company laying off workers. A manager, around 45, with a family and a new house, pleaded to keep his job. Watching him struggle was emotional and showed how real-life challenges affect ordinary people.

Robert has been teaching since 1984 and has noticed one major problem in almost everyone, including himself: self-doubt. People often have talent and knowledge, but fear and lack of confidence hold them back.

He believes that in the real world, success depends on more than grades or knowledge. Courage, boldness, and persistence are what make the difference. Through his experiences as a teacher and a Marine Corps pilot, Robert has seen both sides of human character - the brave and the hesitant.

He stresses that fear can stop people from reaching their potential, especially with money. Robert encourages students to take risks and turn fear into strength. Financial literacy combined with courage gives people more opportunities, even though many prefer to play it safe.

Looking ahead, Robert says the world is changing fast. There will be more inventors like Bell, more innovators like Bill Gates, and many new successful companies. At the same time, there will be layoffs and failures.

He explains why developing financial intelligence is essential. Those who learn to manage money wisely and take smart risks will thrive, while those who don’t may be left behind. Wealth today is in information, not land or factories, and it moves faster than ever. Opportunities are everywhere, but only those ready to act will succeed.

So, what is Robert trying to tell us? To uncover that, just stay tuned!

title: Chapter 4: The History Of Taxes And The Power Of Corporations - Part 3
excerpt: Insights on Kiyosaki's Cashflow Quadrant and wealth building: Chapter 4: The History Of Taxes And The Power Of Corporations - Part 3
content:
Robert reminds us of the first lesson he learned from his Rich Dad when he was 9!

When Robert was just 9 years old, he had to sit and wait for his Rich Dad to decide to talk to him. Rich Dad would purposely ignore him, teaching him to recognize power and to desire it for himself one day.

Throughout the years, Robert learned from him. Rich Dad constantly reminded him that knowledge is power. He explained that money brings power - but only if you know how to use it:

   • If you work for money, you give your power to your employer.

   • If money works for you, you control it and can make it grow.

Rich Dad also warned that the biggest “bully” isn’t the boss - it’s the tax man, who will always take more if you let him.

The main lesson Robert learned was that making money work for you, rather than working for money, is about having and keeping power. Without the right knowledge, the world will push you around.

Once Robert learned the power of money working for him, Rich Dad taught him to be financially smart and not let anyone push him around. Ignorance makes it easy to be bullied, but knowledge gives you a fighting chance. That’s why Rich Dad hired smart tax accountants and attorneys - it was cheaper than paying the government. His key lesson was: “Be smart and you won’t be pushed around.” Knowing the law gave him confidence to stand up for himself.

Robert’s educated father advised him to get a good corporate job and climb the ladder, but Rich Dad laughed: “Why not own the ladder?” As a boy, Robert didn’t fully understand what it meant to own a corporation, it seemed impossible - but the idea excited him. One day, he realized adults could work for a company he might own, showing the power of thinking differently.

It wasn’t until Robert was in his mid-twenties that Rich Dad’s advice truly clicked. After leaving the Marines, he worked for Xerox and earned well, but his paycheck felt small because of huge deductions. Rich Dad’s words echoed in his mind: “Who are you working for? Who are you making rich?”

In 1974, Robert formed his first corporation and focused on building his own assets. By investing his earnings in real estate, he made money work for him instead of working for money. This also made him a better employee, as he worked hard knowing every extra dollar could grow his wealth.

By 1978, he was among Xerox’s top salespeople, but his real wealth came from his own corporation. Cash flow from his properties eventually allowed him to buy his first Porsche, not from spending his paycheck, but from investments. Each dollar in his asset column acted like a hardworking employee, generating more money.

Following Rich Dad’s lessons, Robert escaped the Rat Race early. He calls this financial intelligence, and now he teaches it to others to help them gain control over their money and achieve f inancial freedom.

Robert explains that to build real financial intelligence, you need knowledge in four main areas:

• Accounting - The ability to read and understand numbers and financial statements. This is essential for managing money accurately and spotting the strengths and weaknesses of any business. The more money you handle, the more precise you must be. Accounting is the “left brain” skill that focuses on details.

• Investing - Investing is about making money work for you. It uses strategies and creativity to grow wealth. Instead of working for money, you let your money grow and create more money over time.

• Understanding markets - Markets are driven by supply and demand, as well as emotions. You need to know both the technical side (how markets move) and the fundamental side (economic and financial reasons behind investments) to make smart decisions.

• The law - Corporations, combined with knowledge of accounting, investing, and markets, can help you grow wealth much faster than being an employee or sole proprietor. Understanding tax advantages and legal protections allows the rich to make money work harder and protect their assets.

   • Tax advantages: A corporation can pay business expenses before paying taxes, unlike an employee who gets taxed first and spends what’s left. Even things like car payments, insurance, meals, or vacations can legally become business expenses if done properly.

   • Protection from lawsuits: Corporations and trusts can shield your assets from creditors or lawsuits. Wealthy people often control assets without personally owning them, keeping their wealth protected. In contrast, employees and small business owners who own everything themselves are more exposed.

Robert stresses that financial IQ comes from combining all four skills. Business owners with corporations earn, spend, and then pay taxes, while employees earn, pay taxes, and then spend.

Knowing how to use corporations, taxes, and legal protections effectively can dramatically increase your wealth and security over time.

So, are you ready to see what the next chapter holds for you?

title: Chapter 4: The History Of Taxes And The Power Of Corporations - Part 2
excerpt: Insights on Kiyosaki's Cashflow Quadrant and wealth building: Chapter 4: The History Of Taxes And The Power Of Corporations - Part 2
content:
Here’s a diagram showing how a corporate structure works outside your personal income and expenses.

Robert says the rich get ahead because they understand money and the rules around it, while most poor and middle-class people don’t. He learned this from having two father figures - one socialist and one capitalist, and found that the capitalist ideas made more sense financially. Socialists often struggled because they lacked financial knowledge. No matter what taxes were made to take money from the rich, they always found ways around them, which often left the middle class paying more. The rich succeed because they understand money, which schools don’t teach.

He explains how the rich outsmart intellectuals through government spending. When taxes are collected from the rich, the money goes into the government. Some of it goes to government workers (jobs and pensions), and some goes back to the rich through government contracts. Governments are expected to spend all their money each year or risk losing it. Businesses, however, are praised for saving money. Over time, as government spending grew, taxes eventually affected the poor and middle class - the very people who wanted to tax the rich.

Robert also explains how rich people protect their wealth using corporations. Many people think a corporation is a building or a company, but it’s actually a legal document that creates a separate entity. Corporations help the rich pay less tax and allow some expenses to be paid with pre-tax money.

The struggle between the rich (“haves”) and everyone else (“have-nots”) has existed for hundreds of years. The ones who lose are usually those who work hard and follow the rules but don’t understand how the rich handle money. If people learned these strategies, they could also become financially independent. Robert disagrees with the advice to go to school just for a “safe job,” because employees without financial knowledge remain limited financially.

He says that average Americans work four to five months just to pay taxes. The harder you work, the more you pay. The idea of “take-from-the-rich” often ends up hurting the very people who supported it.

The rich don’t just accept taxes, they fight back. They hire smart lawyers and accountants, influence politicians, and find legal loopholes. The U.S. Tax Code also allows ways to reduce taxes, like Section 1031, which lets people delay paying taxes on real estate gains by trading up to a more expensive property. The rich use these strategies to grow wealth, while most poor and middle-class people miss these opportunities.

Many people pay more taxes or take fewer deductions because they fear the government. Robert has seen businesses unfairly shut down, but the cost of working from January to May just to pay taxes is still very high. His poor dad didn’t fight the system, and his rich dad didn’t either - he just played the game smarter using corporations, which Robert calls the biggest secret of the rich.

Stay tuned to learn more valuable insights!

title: Chapter 4: The History Of Taxes And The Power Of Corporations - Part 1
excerpt: Insights on Kiyosaki's Cashflow Quadrant and wealth building: Chapter 4: The History Of Taxes And The Power Of Corporations - Part 1
content:
“My rich dad just played the game smart, and he did it through corporations - the biggest secret of the rich.”

Robert opens the chapter with this quote, and then explains the story behind it.

He remembers learning about Robin Hood in school. To his teacher, Robin Hood was a hero who took from the rich and gave to the poor. But his rich dad had a very different view - he called Robin Hood a crook. Robert points out that even today, many people still believe in this idea, saying things like, “The rich should pay more taxes.”

The problem, he explains, is that this belief has hurt the poor and the middle class the most. Over time, it’s the middle class - not the rich, who end up paying the highest taxes. To understand why, Robert looks at the history of taxes.

In the early days, both England and America only taxed people temporarily, usually to pay for wars. Britain used taxes to fund its fight against Napoleon, and the U.S. used them during the Civil War. Later, England made income tax permanent in 1874, and the U.S. followed in 1913. At first, taxes were only aimed at the rich, which made the poor and middle class support them. But eventually, the burden shifted, and it was the very people who supported taxes who ended up paying the most.

Rich Dad explained it this way: “Once the government got a taste of money, its appetite grew.” He compared how government workers and business people think. In government, success means spending more money and hiring more people, which makes the organization look bigger. In business, success is the opposite - it’s about spending less, hiring wisely, and creating profit. This is why Rich Dad disliked government growth, it always demanded more tax money.

Robert also shares the clash of ideas he grew up with. His educated dad believed strongly in government programs and even worked in the Peace Corps with his mom, training volunteers for work abroad. He wanted bigger budgets and more people to help society. But his rich dad saw government workers as wasteful, while his poor dad saw the rich as greedy. Robert admits it was confusing to live between these two very different perspectives.

Finally, Rich Dad taught him how the rich play by different rules. They use corporations, a concept that started back in the days of sailing ships. Investors would put money into a company to fund dangerous voyages. If the ship sank, the crew lost their lives, but the investors only lost the money they had put in. This protected their wealth. Rich Dad explained that the same principle still applies today: corporations allow the rich to shield their money and reduce their risk.

Get ready to see how corporations sit outside a personal income statement and balance sheet— explained clearly with a simple diagram!

title: Chapter 3: Mind Your Own Business - Part 2
excerpt: Insights on Kiyosaki's Cashflow Quadrant and wealth building: Chapter 3: Mind Your Own Business - Part 2
content:
Ready to discover more insights from Mind Your Own Business?

One day, Robert tried to get a loan, but his finances didn’t look strong. To appear wealthier, he added all his luxury items - golf clubs, art, electronics, Armani suits, watches, and more - to his assets. Yet, he was still turned down. The problem wasn’t the luxury items - it was that most of his income came from investment real estate, not a “normal” salary. Banks often prefer people who fit the traditional mold.

Robert also warns against focusing on net worth. On paper, it looks impressive, but if you sell your assets, you usually get less, and taxes further reduce the cash. Net worth can often be worth less than it seems.

His advice to us is to focus on real assets that grow in value. Keep your day job, but invest in things that produce income, not personal items like cars or luxury goods. For example, a new car loses about 25% of its value the moment you drive it off the lot. Even Robert’s $400 titanium golf driver dropped to $150 after first use. Bankers may count these items as assets, but in reality, they don’t generate wealth.

So, what assets does Robert recommend for building long-term wealth?

• Businesses you don’t have to run yourself

• Stocks and bonds

• Income-generating real estate

• Notes (IOUs)

• Royalties from intellectual property like music, scripts, or patents

• Anything valuable that generates income or appreciates over time

The key lesson of the chapter is the difference between earning money from a job and creating wealth by building assets. Robert contrasts his “poor dad,” who focused on job security, with his “rich dad,” who encouraged investing in assets you love. He invests in real estate and small company stocks because he enjoys them. Loving what you invest in makes it easier to handle challenges and reduces risk.

Robert advises keeping your day job but also minding your own business by growing your asset column - investments that work for you 24/7, even for future generations.

Another important lesson is that rich people buy luxuries last. They first build assets that generate income. The poor and middle class often do the opposite - they buy luxury items first, usually on credit, which creates debt. For example, Robert’s wife bought a Mercedes only after their real estate investments were generating enough extra cash. This made the car a true reward for financial discipline, not just a fancy purchase.

Starting a company can be exciting, but it’s risky. Most businesses fail. Robert emphasizes that if you don’t truly want to own a company, it’s better to focus on real assets and financial literacy. Learning about cash flow, accounting, and investments will help you analyze opportunities and build long-term wealth.

The big takeaway is to build your assets first, let them generate income, and then enjoy luxuries as a reward. That’s the path to financial intelligence and freedom.

Get ready - next chapter dives even deeper into how to grow your wealth smarter!

title: Chapter 3: Mind Your Own Business - Part 1
excerpt: Insights on Kiyosaki's Cashflow Quadrant and wealth building: Chapter 3: Mind Your Own Business - Part 1
content:
Are you building your assets or just chasing a paycheck? Chapter 3 of Rich Dad Poor Dad, titled “Mind Your Own Business,” explores this important question.

Robert begins the chapter with the inspiring story of Ray Kroc, the man who turned McDonald’s into a global empire.

In 1974, Ray Kroc - the driving force behind McDonald’s success - spoke to MBA students at the University of Texas. After his talk, he joined them for drinks and asked, “What business am I in?”

The students laughed and replied, “You’re in the hamburger business!”

Ray smiled and said, “No, I’m in the real estate business.”

He explained that while McDonald’s sells hamburgers and franchises, the real secret to its success was carefully choosing and owning the land where each restaurant was built. This strategy turned McDonald’s into one of the largest real estate owners in the world, with valuable locations everywhere.

Most people spend their lives working for others - first for their employer, then for the government through taxes, and finally for the bank through loans or mortgages.

The key lesson here, which Robert calls Secret #3 of the Rich, is this: Mind your own business. Financial struggles often happen because people spend their entire lives working for someone else and end up with very little by retirement.

Schools teach us to get jobs and earn money, but they rarely teach us how to build assets. There’s a big difference between your profession and your business. For example, someone might say, “I’m a banker,” but if they don’t own the bank, it’s just their job - not their business. To build wealth, you must focus on creating something that belongs to you, like investments or income-generating assets, instead of working only for a paycheck.

The problem with school is that people often become what they study - chefs, lawyers, mechanics - but forget to “mind their own business.” This means they spend their lives making someone else rich.

To become financially secure, you need to focus on building your asset column, not just increasing your income. The rich buy assets (things that make money), while most people focus only on their salary. That’s why we often hear people say, “I need a raise” or “I need a better job.” These may help - but only if the extra money is used to buy assets.

The poor and middle class often avoid risks because they have no financial safety net. They rely completely on their jobs. When they lose their job, they discover that what they thought were “assets” - their house, car, or expensive items - actually cost them money instead of making them money.

True financial security comes from owning assets that generate income, not just from working harder or earning a higher salary.

Stay tuned for more impactful insights like this!

title: Chapter 2: Why Teach Financial Literacy - Part 7
excerpt: Insights on Kiyosaki's Cashflow Quadrant and wealth building: Chapter 2: Why Teach Financial Literacy - Part 7
content:
Ever wondered who really benefits from all the work we do as employees and homeowners?

We work for the company: Most of what we do ends up making the business owner or shareholders richer, not us. Our effort builds their success more than ours.

We work for the government: A big chunk of our paycheck goes straight to taxes before we even see it. The harder we work, the more we pay.

We work for the bank: After taxes, much of what’s left goes to mortgages, loans, or credit cards. The bank benefits from our work just as much as the company and the government.

The problem with simply working harder is that most of the extra effort ends up benefiting the company, the government, and the bank - not us. True financial growth comes from focusing on efforts that directly benefit ourselves and our families.

By “minding our own business” - focusing on acquiring assets instead of chasing a bigger paycheck - you can gradually build wealth, even while keeping a regular job. As assets grow, it becomes important to measure success and understand when you are truly wealthy.

Robert also introduces personal definitions for assets, liabilities, and wealth, borrowing from R. Buckminster Fuller - a thinker known for his geodesic dome but also for his unique perspective on what real wealth means.

Wealth isn’t just about what we own - it’s about how long we could survive if we stopped working today. Unlike net worth, which counts all our assets minus liabilities (including things we own that may not make money), wealth focuses on the money our money is actually earning.

This helps us understand our finances better and see how near we are to true financial independence. Instead of counting the things we own, it focuses on how long we could live off our income from assets.

Robert points out that wealth isn’t just about the money we earn from working - it’s about how much our assets earn compared to our expenses.

For example, if our assets generate $1,000 a month but our expenses are $2,000, we can only cover half a month. True wealth comes when our assets generate enough income to cover all our expenses.

Once we reach that point, we are financially independent. We don’t need a paycheck because our assets pay for our lifestyle. Any extra income can be reinvested to buy more assets, creating even more cash flow. As Robert emphasizes:

• The rich buy assets.

• The poor only have expenses.

• The middle class buy things they think are assets but are really liabilities.

So, focus on building assets, keep expenses lower than asset income, and we move toward wealth and financial freedom.

Get ready to discover what the next chapter holds for us!

title: Chapter 2: Why Teach Financial Literacy - Part 6
excerpt: Insights on Kiyosaki's Cashflow Quadrant and wealth building: Chapter 2: Why Teach Financial Literacy - Part 6
content:
Want to know why the rich keep getting richer?

When we look at Rich Dad’s financial statement, we can see why the rich get richer.

• His assets bring in a lot of income.

• That income is more than enough to pay for all his expenses.

• The extra money is then put back into buying more assets.

• Because of this, his asset column keeps growing.

• As the assets grow, the income they produce also grows.

That’s why the rich keep getting richer - their money is working for them all the time.

Let’s see why the middle class struggles!

The middle class keeps struggling with money because:

• They mostly earn from their salary.

• When their salary goes up, their taxes also go up.

• At the same time, their expenses rise as they spend more.

• They buy a house and call it an asset, but it doesn’t actually put money in their pocket.

• Since they don’t invest in real assets that make money, they stay stuck in the “rat race.”

Many middle-class people think a pay raise means they can buy a bigger house or spend more. But this habit creates debt and financial stress. Even if they keep getting raises, their spending and debt also grow, making their lives risky. This happens mainly because they lack financial education.

We can see how fragile the middle class is:

• Jobs are no longer secure.

• Pensions are being replaced by retirement plans like 401(k).

• Social Security cannot be fully trusted.

• Families are stuck paying mortgages, taxes, credit cards, and saving for children’s college.

To feel “safe,” many invest in mutual funds. They believe mutual funds reduce risk because the money is spread out (diversified). But in truth, they do this because they don’t have the time or knowledge to study real investing.

The problem is the weak financial education:

• They call liabilities (like homes, loans, and cars) "assets."

• They have one income, their paycheck.

• They have heavy debt and few to no income-producing assets.

• So they can't take great investment opportunities when they come.

The solution is simple:

• Understand the difference in an asset and liability.

• Buy income-producing assets.

• Keep expenses and liabilities low.

As an employee and a homeowner, do you realize how your working efforts are generally used? Stay tuned to know that!

title: Chapter 2: Why Teach Financial Literacy - Part 5
excerpt: Insights on Kiyosaki's Cashflow Quadrant and wealth building: Chapter 2: Why Teach Financial Literacy - Part 5
content:
Want to see the diagram that explains mortgage payments and why they go straight into the expense column? Here it is!

Robert recalls drawing a diagram for his dad to show how money moves. He highlighted the extra costs that come with owning a home. The bigger the house, the higher the expenses - so most of the money kept flowing out.

He often gets challenged when he says a house is not an asset. For many, a home is their dream and their largest investment. Robert agrees that owning a home is better than nothing, but he offers a different perspective.

He explains that if he and his wife bought a bigger, flashier house, it wouldn’t be an asset - it would be a liability. Money would keep leaving their pockets for mortgage payments, property taxes, insurance, maintenance, and utilities.

Robert also points out that people struggle to see this because a home is emotional. When emotions are high, financial intelligence often drops. He says money has a way of making decisions emotional.

Buying a house that’s too expensive instead of starting an investment portfolio can hurt a person in three ways:

1. Loss of time – time that could have grown other assets.

2. Loss of money – cash that could have been invested goes to home expenses.

3. Loss of education – when all money is tied up in a house, savings, or retirement, people don’t invest. Without investing, they miss out on experience and rarely become sophisticated investors. The best opportunities usually go first to those who are.

Robert clarifies: he’s not saying don’t buy a house. He’s saying you need to understand the difference between an asset and a liability.

He explains that when he wants a bigger house, he first buys assets that generate enough cash flow to pay for it.

He uses his educated dad as an example of someone stuck in the Rat Race: his dad’s expenses matched his income, leaving nothing to invest. As a result, his liabilities were higher than his assets.

In contrast, his rich dad’s financial statement shows what happens when you focus on investing and minimizing liabilities. It’s a clear lesson in the power of smart financial choices.

Curious why the rich keep getting richer? Stay tuned!

title: Chapter 2: Why Teach Financial Literacy - Part 4
excerpt: Insights on Kiyosaki's Cashflow Quadrant and wealth building: Chapter 2: Why Teach Financial Literacy - Part 4
content:
Ready to find out why the dream of financial freedom turns into a nightmare for so many?

Robert tells a story - let’s explore it to uncover valuable money lessons!

The story of hardworking people often follows a familiar pattern. A newly married, well-educated young couple moves into a small rented apartment. They quickly notice that living together is cheaper than living alone.

The apartment is cramped, so they start saving money to buy their dream home where they can raise children. With two incomes, they focus on their careers, and their earnings begin to grow.

But as their incomes rise, their expenses rise too.

For most people, the biggest expense is taxes. Many think it’s income tax, but for most Americans, the highest tax is actually Social Security. As an employee, it looks like you pay about 7.5% for Social Security and Medicare, but the real cost is 15% because your employer has to match the Social Security amount. In other words, it’s money your employer could have paid you. On top of that, you still pay income tax on the part of your wages taken out for Social Security, money you never even received.

Going back to the young couple, as their incomes rise, they decide to buy their dream home. Once they move in, they face a new tax: property tax. Then they buy a new car, furniture, and appliances to match their new home. Before they know it, their liabilities grow with a mortgage and credit-card debt.

The young couple is now trapped in the “Rat Race.” Soon, a baby comes along, and they work even harder. Their higher incomes mean higher taxes, called “bracket creep.” A credit card arrives in the mail - they use it and max it out.

Then a loan company notices that their house has increased in value. Because their credit is good, they are offered a bill-consolidation loan to pay off high-interest credit cards. They go for it, feeling smart, and roll their credit card debt into their mortgage. Monthly payments drop because the debt is spread over 30 years. It seems like the right thing to do.

But then life goes on. The neighbour invites them shopping, sales are on, and another credit card comes along “just in case.”

Many people think more money will solve their problems, but often the real problem is how they spend it. Financial illiteracy, like not understanding the difference between an asset and a liability, causes most money troubles.

There’s a saying: “If you find yourself in a hole, stop digging.” Intelligence and self-knowledge are more powerful than just working harder. A lesson from Japanese culture highlights three powers: the sword (force), the jewel (money), and the mirror (self-knowledge). Of the three, self-knowledge is the most valuable.

Most people let money control them. They follow the crowd, do what everyone else does, and ignore their inner wisdom. They repeat advice without thinking: “Your home is an asset,” “Get a loan to pay off debt,” “Work harder,” “Save money,” “Mutual funds are safe.” Fear of standing out or being different keeps people from questioning these ideas.

Rich Dad taught that the key is to use your intelligence, not just follow rules blindly. He showed that success comes from thinking independently, hiring smart people, and understanding money. Mike and Robert learned from him by observing how he ran his business, talked to bankers, lawyers, and investors, and made financial decisions.

By the time Robert was 16, he knew more about money than most adults around him. He could track finances, understand taxes, and learn from professionals. But this knowledge often clashed with what schools taught: good grades, rules, and following the crowd. While schools trained people to be employees, Rich Dad taught them to be financially smart and independent thinkers.

For example, robert’s own father believed their home was the best investment, but Robert questioned that idea. Learning to think critically about money, rather than just accepting common advice, became the foundation for financial freedom.

The diagram above shows how his rich dad and poor dad thought differently about their homes. One saw his house as an asset, the other saw it as a liability.

There’s another diagram that explains mortgage payments and how they become part of the expense column.

Get ready to check it out!

title: Chapter 2: Why Teach Financial Literacy - Part 3
excerpt: Insights on Kiyosaki's Cashflow Quadrant and wealth building: Chapter 2: Why Teach Financial Literacy - Part 3
content:
Ready to learn about the cash flow pattern of a poor person?

These diagrams are simple examples and don’t show everything about real life. Everyone needs money for essentials like food, housing, and clothing. But the diagrams help us understand how money flows in the lives of poor, middle-class, and rich people. This flow of money shows how someone manages their finances.

Robert started with the story of rich people in America to show that money alone doesn’t fix all problems. That’s why he feels uneasy when people ask him how to get rich quickly. Many say, “I’m in debt, so I need to earn more money.”

But earning more money often doesn’t help. In fact, it can make things worse. Money tends to highlight where we lack knowledge. That’s why someone who suddenly receives a large amount of money through an inheritance, a raise, or winning the lottery - can end up broke again, or even worse off.

Money simply reveals your habits. If your habit is to spend everything you earn, then more money will just lead to more spending. That’s why people say, “A fool and his money is one big party.”

Robert often said that school teaches us academic and job-related skills, which are useful and help us earn money. Back in the 1960s, if a student was smart, people assumed they would become a doctor, because doctors were expected to earn the most.

But today, doctors face many financial challenges - insurance companies, government rules, and lawsuits. Now, kids want to be famous athletes, movie stars, or CEOs, because those roles seem exciting, well-paid, and prestigious. That’s one reason why it’s harder to motivate students in school today.

People are beginning to realize that doing well in school doesn’t always lead to success in life or in a career, as it once did.

Many students leave school without learning how to handle money. So even though they may do well in their jobs, they often struggle financially. They work harder and harder but still don’t seem to get ahead.

The problem isn’t that they don’t know how to earn money - it’s that they were never taught how to manage it. This skill is called financial aptitude. It means knowing what to do with your money once you earn it - how to protect it, how to keep it, and how to make it grow by working for you.

Most people don’t understand why they have money problems. The real reason is that they don’t understand cash flow - how money moves in and out of their lives.

Someone can be very smart and successful in their job, but still not know how to manage money. These people often work harder than they need to because they learned how to work for money - not how to make money work for them.

Want to see how the dream of financial freedom becomes a nightmare for many?

Stay tuned!

title: Chapter 2: Why Teach Financial Literacy - Part 2
excerpt: Insights on Kiyosaki's Cashflow Quadrant and wealth building: Chapter 2: Why Teach Financial Literacy - Part 2
content:
Ready to Learn What the KISS Principle Is?

Rich Dad liked to keep things simple. He believed in the "KISS" rule: Keep It Simple, Stupid (or Keep It Super Simple). Because he explained money in a straightforward way, it helped others understand better and build a strong foundation for their financial future.

But here’s the problem: Most people are confused about money.

Why? Because we’re often taught the wrong way - or not taught at all. We learn basic reading and writing (literacy), but not financial literacy (how money really works).

For example, many people confuse assets and liabilities. Even the dictionary can make these terms more confusing. But Rich Dad made it simple:

• An asset puts money in your pocket.

• A liability takes money out of your pocket.

That’s it.

He used to say, “Don’t just go by the words - look at the numbers. If you can’t understand the numbers, you won’t know if you own something valuable or just a hole in the ground.”

Numbers tell a story, just like words do. If you want to be rich, you need to understand that story. Rich Dad always said:

“The rich buy assets. The poor and middle class buy liabilities - thinking they’re assets.”

Even though some professionals may not agree with his definitions, this simple diagram helped Robert and Mike, even as kids, learn how money really works - and it became the starting point of their financial success.

Here’s that simple diagram:

This is the cash-flow pattern of an asset:

Let’s keep it easy to understand.

When you look at your money, there are two important parts:

1. The Income Statement (Top Part)

This shows your income (money coming in) and your expenses (money going out). It tells you if you're making or losing money.

2. The Balance Sheet (Bottom Part)

This shows your assets (things that put money in your pocket) and your liabilities (things that take money out). It's called a "balance" sheet because it gives a clear picture of what you own vs. what you owe.

Most people don’t realize these two are connected.

For example:

• If you buy an asset, like a rental property, it puts money into your income.

• If you buy a liability, like a new car on a loan, it creates more expenses.

This is the cash-flow pattern of a liability:

Now that you’ve seen what assets and liabilities look like in pictures, let’s talk about them in words.

If you want to become rich, spend your life buying assets.

If you want to stay poor or stuck in the middle class, keep buying liabilities.

Why do most people struggle with money?

Because they don’t truly understand money - in words or in numbers. This is called financial illiteracy - not knowing how money works.

Rich people are usually rich because they’ve learned how to understand money better than others.

If you want to become rich - you need to become financially literate, meaning you understand money in both words and numbers.

Those arrows in the diagram show how money moves. That’s called cash flow - how cash flows in or out.

Just like random words don’t tell you much without a story, numbers alone don’t mean much either. What matters is what the numbers are telling you - the story behind them.

When you look at someone’s financial report or money situation, it tells a story. And sadly, for about 80% of families, the story is:

“We work hard, but we never seem to get ahead.”

Why? Because most of their money goes into liabilities, not assets.

And that’s the problem.

So, what does the cash flow pattern of a poor person look like?

Get ready to find out!

title: Chapter 2: Why Teach Financial Literacy - Part 1
excerpt: Insights on Kiyosaki's Cashflow Quadrant and wealth building: Chapter 2: Why Teach Financial Literacy - Part 1
content:
It’s not about how much money you make - it’s about how much you keep.

Robert shares how learning about money helped both him and his friend Mike become successful. Mike took over his father’s business and grew it even more. Robert chose a different path - he retired at age 47 because his investments were making money on their own. It was like planting a tree and watching it grow big enough to give shade without needing more care.

When people ask Robert how to become rich, he always gives the same advice that his “rich dad” gave him:

“If you want to be rich, you need to understand money.”

That simply means: Learn how money really works.

Back in 1923, some of the richest and most powerful businessmen in America met in Chicago. But just 25 years later, most of them had lost everything - some went bankrupt, some went to jail, and sadly, some even died by suicide.

The takeaway? Making a lot of money doesn’t mean you’ll stay rich. If you don’t know how to manage it, you could lose it all.

Even today, things change fast. The economy goes up and down. Without financial knowledge, these changes can hurt your finances. That’s why learning is more powerful than just earning.

Why Some People Lose Their Wealth?

You’ve probably heard stories about lottery winners or famous athletes who made millions - and then went broke. Why does this happen?

Because they didn’t learn how to manage their money. They knew how to earn it, but not how to keep or grow it. One sad example is a young basketball player who lost everything and had to work in a car wash to survive.

So once again, the key message is: It’s not how much money you make. It’s how much you keep and what you do with it.

Most schools don’t teach students how to handle money. They focus on academic subjects but leave out real-life skills like budgeting, saving, and investing.

It’s like trying to build a tall skyscraper on soft ground with no solid foundation. Without financial knowledge, people try to get rich quickly, but their “financial house” falls apart. They end up stressed, broke, and in debt.

Robert and Mike were different. They learned about money from a young age, thanks to Mike’s dad - who they called “rich dad.” He used drawings and simple lessons to help them understand how money works. That strong foundation helped them succeed as adults.

Rule #1: Learn the Difference Between Assets and Liabilities - and Focus on Buying Assets

If you want to become wealthy, this is the most important rule to follow: Buy assets, not liabilities.

This rule sounds easy, but most people don’t really understand it. That’s why so many struggle f inancially.

Rich dad always said: “Rich people buy assets. Poor and middle-class people buy liabilities thinking they’re assets.”

When he first explained this to Robert and Mike, they were confused. They expected some complicated secret. But this simple rule surprised them.

Robert asked, “Is it really that easy? Just buy assets to get rich?”

Rich dad said, “Yes, it’s that simple.”

Then Robert asked, “If it’s so simple, why isn’t everyone rich?”

Rich dad replied, “Because most people don’t actually know what an asset or a liability is.”

So, What’s an Asset? What’s a Liability?

Here’s how rich dad explained it in the simplest way possible:

• An asset is something that puts money into your pocket.

• A liability is something that takes money out of your pocket.

That’s it. Very simple.

But many people are taught complicated definitions that make it hard to understand. Bankers, accountants, and financial experts often use technical terms, and people end up confused. So they make poor decisions, buying things that actually hurt their finances.

Rich dad believed in keeping things simple. He followed the KISS rule!

Get ready to find out what it was!

title: Chapter 1: The Rich Dont Work For Money - Part 4
excerpt: Insights on Kiyosaki's Cashflow Quadrant and wealth building: Chapter 1: The Rich Dont Work For Money - Part 4
content:
Rich Dad was trying to teach the boys an important life lesson. He told them that most people fall into a trap:

They work hard all their lives for little money, believing their job gives them security. They live for small things like a short vacation each year or a small retirement check after decades of work.

He wasn't making fun of them - he actually cared. But he wanted the boys to see a bigger truth that most people miss. He wanted to open their eyes.

To test them, Rich Dad offered more and more money - first 25 cents, then $1, then $2, and finally $5 an hour. He wanted to see if they would give in to temptation.

At first, the boys were excited. The thought of making that kind of money made their hearts race. But eventually, they stayed quiet and refused. That’s when Rich Dad knew they were ready to learn.

He explained:

• Most people are controlled by fear (of not having money) and greed (the desire for more stuff).

• That fear pushes them to work hard, and the greed makes them spend their paycheck quickly.

• This creates a never-ending loop: Work - Get paid - Spend - Repeat.

• He called this the "Rat Race."

Rich Dad told them there is another way - a smarter path to freedom - but only a few people ever f ind it. The first step? Being honest with yourself and breaking free from the emotions that trap most people.

Rich dad explained that most people let their emotions control how they think, especially when it comes to money. The two strongest emotions are fear and desire. People feel afraid of not having money, so they go to work to earn it. Then, when they get paid, they feel happy and excited. That excitement leads to desire - they want more things, nicer things, better things. But soon after, the fear comes back again. So they go back to work. This cycle of fear and desire keeps repeating, and most people stay stuck in it their entire lives.

The problem is, they never stop to think about what's really happening. They just react to their emotions. Fear tells them to get a job. Desire pushes them to spend their money. And then fear returns, making them feel they need to earn more. It's like being on a hamster wheel - running in circles but never getting anywhere.

Rich dad said this is why most people don’t become wealthy. It’s not because they’re not smart or don’t work hard. It’s because they don’t learn how to control their emotions. Instead of using their brains to think clearly, they let fear and desire make decisions for them.

Even people who become rich can fall into this trap. They may have a lot of money, but they’re still afraid of losing it. Or they keep wanting more because they think it will make them happy. So even though they look successful, they’re still controlled by the same emotions.

Some people pretend that money doesn’t matter to them, but they still spend most of their lives working for it. That’s called denial - saying one thing but doing the opposite. Others spend recklessly when they feel excited, thinking that buying more things will make them feel better. But it never lasts.

Rich dad said the key to escaping this trap is learning about money. Schools teach people how to work for money, but they don’t teach how money actually works. As a result, most people become slaves to their jobs and their paychecks. They don’t realize that money is just an idea - not something to fear or chase blindly.

The first step to real freedom is becoming aware of your emotions. It’s okay to feel fear or desire, but don’t let those feelings control your choices. Instead, use your brain to think things through. Ask smart questions. Don’t just react. That’s how you stop being a slave to money - and start learning to make money work for you.

In this case, they didn’t get paid. They worked in a small store for free. Most people would’ve quit right there. But not them.

Instead, they listened to Rich Dad’s advice:

“Don’t depend on a paycheck. Use your brain. Work for free - and you’ll start seeing what others miss.”

So they watched. They paid attention. And one day, they noticed something odd: the store lady was cutting comic book covers off and throwing the rest in the trash.
